HAMLEY, WYMOND.—Appointed collector of customs for the colony of British Columbia, Sept. 1858; was a member of the executive and legislative council; collector of customs of Victoria.

HAMPTON, (Unit. Kgd., 1874), Bart. (Unit. Kgd. 1846.) Sir John Somerset Pakington, 1st Baron, P.C., G.C.B. (1859.) Was educated at Eton and at Oriel College, Oxford; was secretary of state for the colonies from Feb. to Dec. 1852, was first lord of the admiralty, Feb. 1858, to June 1859; became a deputy-lieut. of Worcestershire in 1832; major

Worcestershire yeomanry cavalry, 1856; was chairman of the quarter sessions of that county from 1834 to 1854. Was M.P. for Droitwich from 1837 to 1874; first lord of the admiralty, July, 1866; afterwards secretary of state for war till 9th Dec. 1868; civil service commissioner, Nov., 1875.

HAMPSHIRE, FREDERICK K., M.B., M.R.C.S., London.—Appointed a colonial surgeon, S.S., July 14th, 1868. On arrival in the straits, was appointed resident surgeon at Malacca with medical charge of native troops in garrison there. Was transferred to Singapore in May, 1872. Is in charge of general hospital, in medical charge of civil and criminal gaols. Police surgeon and deputy superintendent of vaccination. Is also government analyst for the Straits Settlements.

HANCOCK, HENRY JAMES BURFORD.—Educated at Eton; served in her Majesty's 45th regiment (Sherwood Foresters), and a captain in the Kent artillery militia; called to the bar, Inner Temple, January, 1866; received medal from H.I.M. Napoleon III., for "Treatise on International Fishery Laws," 1866; district judge in Jamaica, 1st May, 1876; appointed to Falmouth district, 10th August, 1876.
HANSARD, LIEUT. A.—Officer commanding pioneer force, public works department, Ceylon, 1868; superintendent of police, May, 1873.
HANSON, A. B.—Was appointed tide waiter, 1862; landing surveyor, Sierra Leone, 15th Oct., 1863.

HARE, GUSTAVUS EDWARD COCKBURN.—Was educated at Winchester College; served in the 7th regiment of Prussian Lancers; was appointed superintendent of land police in Western Australia in 1867; resident magistrate of Albany district, May, 1871.
HARECOURT, FREDERICK.—Appointed inspector of police, British Guiana, 1873.

HARGRAVE, JOHN FLETCHER.—Called to the bar, Lincoln's Inn, Jan. 1841, Q.C. and solicitor-general of New South Wales, M.L.C., representing the Cowper government in the legislative council, May 27th, 1862; attorney-general, New South Wales, 1864; 3rd puisne judge, 1865; senior puisne judge.

HARMAN, GEORGE C.—Was employed from May 1870, until March 1874, in the Receiver and Accountant General's Branch, General Post Office, London; appointed Assistant Postmaster Singapore Straits Settlements in July, 1874.

HARLEY, COLONEL ROBERT WILLIAM, C.B (1867), C.M.G. (1874).—Administered the government of Honduras from 21st June, 1871, to 9th June, 1872; administrator of the Gold Coast, September, 1872, until 1874; lieut.-governor, Tobago, September, 1875.

HARRAGIN, JOHN ARTHUR.—Entered the civil service of Trinidad in 1861, as supernumerary in the colonial secretary's office; in 1862, appointed assistant clerk of the peace for Port of Spain; in 1864, to act as sixth clerk in the receiver general's office; in the same year, clerk to the inspector commandant of police; end of 1865, chief clerk of the colonial hospital; in 1867, appointed landing waiter, customs department; in 1871, inspector of police, northern division; in 1873, acting inspector of police, southern division; in the same year, warder, &c., of the Couva Ward Union, and took over the duties on the 1st September, 1874; acting inspector of immigrants in 1875; and a justice of the peace for the district.
HARRISON, ROBERT ALEXANDER, D.C.L.—Born, 1833; called to the bar, Upper Canada, 1855; created a Q.C., 1867; chief clerk of the
Digitized by Google
Y 2
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

crown law department for Upper Canada, from 1854 to 1859; sat in House of Commons from 1867 to 1872, and while there was chairman of the standing committee on miscellaneous and private bills; author of "Harrison and Robinson's Digest"; "The Municipal Manual," &c.; appointed chief justice of Ontario, 8th October, 1875.

HART, ANTHONY DE L'LANO.—Entered the civil service at Trinidad as storekeeper, October 4th, 1866; office of the court of intendant, 1868; office of stipendiary magistrate, Port of Spain, 1868; marshal's office, June, 1869; clerk at Government House, 21st Sept., 1869; promoted to the customs department in 1871; appointed landing waiter, 1872, and inspector of Venezuelan vessels, in addition to other duties, 1876.

HART, DANIEL MAC DONNELL.—Entered the Trinidad civil service as clerk of police, 1st Jan., 1862; clerk, royal gaol, 22nd August, 1863; department of crown lands and maps and surveys, 28th Nov., 1864; immigration office, 17th Oct., 1865; second clerk of the peace, 10th March, 1866; clerk of the peace, Couva, 1st May, 1868; promoted as clerk of the peace for Cedros, La Brea, and Guapo, 1st Feb., 1870; secretary to the board of education, 1st July, 1871; clerk, colonial secretary's department, 7th August, 1871; is secretary to the council of the Queen's royal college; and secretary to the reformatory and industrial school committee.

HART, FREDERICK RALPH, F.R.G.S., F.R.A.S., &c.—Appointed, on the 1st October, 1873, clerk of the peace for the western district, county of St. Patrick (Cedros, La Brea, and Guapo), and clerk of the petty civil courts comprised in the above-mentioned places; is author of "India Bound to Indo-China," "London Bound," "Scrambles in Venezuela," "Notes on the Suez Canal," taken by him on the spot in February, 1872, and published in 1876, &c.; is a member of the geographical society of Paris. Appointed, on the 1st July, 1874, clerk of the peace for Savanna Grande, in the county of Victoria, also for Oropouche, La Brea, and Guapo (circuit), in the western district of the county of St. Patrick, and clerk of the petty civil courts comprised in the above-mentioned districts; is author of a map of Venezuela on the largest scale yet produced, which was drawn by him after his survey in that country in 1872, and now at the geographical society's rooms, Paris; is a French and Spanish interpreter.

HARTSHORNE, B. F.—Educated at Charterhouse and Pembroke College, Oxford, where he obtained a scholarship, 1863; graduated with classical honours B.A. and S.C.L., 1868; appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, Jan., 1869; police magistrate, Galagadara, April, 1871; acting police magistrate, Dumbara, July, 1871; acting assistant government agent, Nuwera Eliya, June, 1872.

HARVEST, LIEUT.-COLONEL EDWARD DOUGLAS.—Entered the army in 1842; served in the Mediterranean, North America, and India; appointed resident of Cerigo, Ionian Islands, July, 1860, relinquished the appointment November, 1861, in order to join his regiment in India; appointed commandant, Western Australia, 12th January, 1872; is a member of the executive council of that colony; holds her Majesty's commission to administer the government in the event of the governor's death or absence; administered the government of the colony from 12th January to 1st April, 1874, during Governor Weld's absence on leave.
HATCHELL, DAVID THOMPSON.—Captain Madras Staff Corps, educated at Rugby, entered the army as Ensign in the 2nd Madras European Light Infantry, June 12th, 1858, was attached to H.M.'s

44th Regiment from Sept., 1858, till June 1859; exchanged into the 34th Madras Light Infantry; Lieut. Sept., 1860; joined the Engineering College at Madras in Jan., 1863; obtained a certificate in surveying; rejoined the 34th Regiment at Singapore, June, 1863; appointed adjutant of the Regiment; joined the Madras Staff Corps 1865; appointed Supt. officer Wellesley, 1866; acted twice as Lieut. Governor of Penang, 1866, and 1871; Captain June, 1870; transferred to Penang as police magistrate, and Commissioner Court of Requests, 1873.

HAUGHTON, S.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, Sept., 1870; acting police magistrate, Panadura, June, 1872; police magistrate, Haldummulla, and acting assistant government agent, Kandy Sept., 1872; assistant government agent, Anuradhapura, Sept., 1873, to continue to act at Kandy.

HAVELOCK, ARTHUR ELIBANK.—Entered the army, 32nd Light Infantry, Jan. 1862; captain, 1873; private secretary and aide-de-camp to the officer administering the government of Mauritius from July to November, 1873; acting chief civil commissioner, Seychelles Islands, from April, 1874, until February, 1875; appointed member of the executive and legislative councils of Fiji, May, 1875; appointed (provisionally) colonial secretary and receiver-general, Fiji, 1st September, 1875; president of Nevis, 1877.
HAWKER, GEORGE C., M.A., Trinity College, Cambridge.—Speaker of the house of assembly, South Australia, 1860; re-elected, ditto, 1863; chief secretary, 1876.

HAWKINS, A. CÆSAR.—Entered the army as ensign in the 1st royals, Feb., 1836, and retired after 12 years' service; appointed resident magistrate of the Weenen, Natal, Sept., 1852, and removed to resident magistracy of the Upper Umcomas division of that colony, Feb., 1855; was a major in the Natal carbineers.

HAWTAYNE, GEORGE HAMMOND.—Police magistrate, Kingstown district, St. Vincent, Jan. 1, 1872; was private secretary to Mr. Eyre, when lieut.-governor of St. Vincent, in 1854; appointed police and stipendiary magistrate of that colony, March, 1857; was a member of assembly; commanded from 1863 to 1869, the Queen's volunteer corps, which was raised by him; private secretary to the governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands from 1869 to Dec. 1871; acted as colonial secretary of St. Vincent in 1871 and in 1874.

HAY, SIR E. HAY DRUMMOND, KNT. BACH. (creat. 1859).—Entered the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 1832; acted for some time as private secretary to Mr. Hay, under secretary of state; appointed president administering the government of the Virgin Islands, Feb. 1839; lieut.-governor of St. Kitts, Sept. 1850; lieut.-governor of the Leeward Islands, 1852; and governor and vice-admiral of St. Helena, March, 1855, to July, 1863; retired on pension, 1867.

HAY, CAPTAIN, J. S.—Assistant inspector Gold Coast constabulary; joined 89th Princess Victoria's regiment in 1858, served with that regiment in India during the latter part of the Mutiny; served as adjutant for some time, and afterwards as instructor of musketry; was acting adjutant to the provisional depot battalion at Dum Dum in 1859; appointed adjutant to the Glasgow Highland Volunteers in 1868 at their formation; and received special notice from the assistant adjutant-general of the reserve forces for the state of efficiency to which the corps had been brought; also received the thanks of the colonel and officers for the same;
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
appointed assistant inspector Gold Coast armed native police, 1875.
HAY, C. S.—Deputy Queen's advocate, Eastern Province, Ceylon, 1868.
HAYNES, ROBERT. — Registrar in chancery, clerk of the crown; prothonotary and clerk of common pleas, Barbados.
HAYWARD, JOHN.—Solicitor-general, Newfoundland; assistant-judge 1868.
HAYWOOD, D. A.—Appointed clerk of courts, Cape Coast Castle, June, 1868.
HAZELAND, F. J.—Crown solicitor, Hong Kong; appointed 1861.
HEAGAN, R. S.—Excise officer, Antigua, 1868; clerk of vestries, secretary to board of health, &c.
HECTOR, JAMES, M.D., C.M.G., (1875).—director of geological surveys and curator of the colonial museum, New Zealand.

HELDEN, MAJOR WILLIAM AUGUSTUS TRYDEN C.M.G., (1874) 100th regiment of infantry.—Appointed to the Order of St. Michael and St. George for special service on the Gold Coast during the Ashantee War, 1873-4.
HELMICH, A.—Postmaster-general, Western Australia; appointed 1847; was clerk to the auditor-general of the colony from 1843 to 1847.
HELPS, E. A.—Appointed inspector of schools, Ceylon, November, 1870; inspector of schools in England, 1875.

HEMMING, A. W. L.—Appointed, after competitive examination, a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1860; promoted Oct. 1864, 1st junior class; private secretary to Sir F. Rogers (now Lord Blachford), 20th May, 1867, till 20th May, 1871; private secretary to Mr. Herbert, 21st May, 1871, till 29th September, 1872; appointed a 2nd class clerk, 30th September, 1872; 1st class clerk November, 1874.

HEMMING, JOHN.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Albert division, Cape Colony, appointed 30th May, 1868; was clerk to C. C. and R. M., Queenstown, from 1860 to 1868; ditto at Bathurst, 1858 to 1860; ditto at Oudtshoorn, 1858; clerk to R. M. Oudtshoorn, 1857; C. C. and R. M., King William's Town, 1873.

HENDERSON, A.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Murraysburg division, Cape Colony; appointed 1859. Was magistrate's clerk for Victoria district, July 1848 to 1854; clerk to the civil commissioner at Albert, Aug. 1854; clerk to civil commissioner, Victoria, East, April 1856; and acting civil commissioner, 1857.

HENNESSY, JOHN POPE, C.M.G. (1872), F.G.S., F.R.A.S., Knight of Malta.—Sat in the House of Commons from 1859 to 1865, as M.P. for the King's county; was called to the bar at the Inner Temple; is a county magistrate for Ireland; was appointed in April, 1867, governor of Labuan, and consul-general for Borneo; appointed, Dec. 1871, governor of the Bahamas; employed under the colonial office at the Hague respecting the transfer to the British crown of the Netherlands possessions in Africa; appointed, Jan. 1872, administrator-in-chief of the West Africa Settlements. Received the C.M.G. for services connected with the transfer of Elmina; governor of the Bahamas, May, 1873; governor of the Windward Islands, 1875; governor of Hong Kong, 1877.

HENRY, W. A.—Called to the bar, Nova Scotia, 1841; Q.C., 1849; member of executive council, 1845, subsequently solicitor-general, attorney-general, and financial secretary; appointed a puisne judge of the supreme court of the Dominion, 8th October, 1875.
HERBERT, ROBERT G. W., D.C.L. (Oxon.)—

Educated at Eton, and at Balliol College, Oxford, of which he was elected Scholar in 1849; Hertford Scholar, 1851; Ireland Scholar, 1852; Latin Verse Prize, 1852; Eldon Scholar, 1854; elected Fellow of All Souls, 1854; private secretary to the chancellor of the exchequer (the Right Hon. W. E. Gladstone), 1855; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1858; appointed in 1859, colonial secretary of Queensland; premier of Queensland and member of legislative assembly from 1860 to 1865; one of the assistant secretaries to the Board of Trade, from 1868 to Feb. 1870; when he was appointed assistant under-secretary of state for the colonies; appointed under-secretary of state for the colonies, 21 May, 1871; is a magistrate and deputy lieutenant of the county of Cambridge.
HERBERT, W. H.—Appointed assistant government printer, Ceylon, 1854; acting government printer, 1864; and government printer, March, 1872.
HERCHENRODER, L.—Appointed first clerk in the registration and mortgage department, Mauritius, April, 1862.

HERVEY, D. F. A.—Appointed, after competitive examination, to the civil service, Straits Settlements, 1868; acting sheriff of Singapore, 1870; acting police magistrate, 1871; senior sworn clerk, 1871.
HEWARD, ROBERT, F.L.S.—Entered colonial office, May, 1852; appointed index compiler, Oct. 1859; clerk in the general department, July, 1870; retired, 29th September, 1876.
HEWET, JOHN.—Appointed an engineer in the harbour-master's department at Mauritius, Sept., 1851.

HICKSON, RICHARD MAHONEY.—Educated at Harrow; entered the army as ensign in the 79th regiment in 1844; served with the British forces in Monte Video in 1846-7, and in the Cape war 1847-8, for which latter service he received a medal, afterwards served in the Ceylon rifles and 40th regiment; was captain in the royal Glamorganshire light infantry regiment of militia; appointed president of the Virgin Islands, June, 1873.

HIGGINSON, K.C.B. (Civil; creat. 1857.)— SIR JAMES MACAULAY.—Was for some years private secretary to Lord Metcalfe, in Jamaica and Canada; was governor of Antigua and the Leeward Islands, also governor of Mauritius, 1850 to 1857.

HILL, COL. SIR STEPHEN JOHN, K.C.M.G. (1874), C.B.—Entered the army, Nov. 10, 1825; was promoted lieut. Feb. 13, 1828; a captain, April 15, 1842; commanded an expedition 80 miles up the Gambia in 1849; stormed and destroyed the fortified town of Keenung, May 7, and was present at the action and defeat of the enemy on the plains of Queenella; he also commanded a detachment of the 2nd and 3rd West India regiments in the combined attack of a British and French naval and land force under Commodore Fanshawe, in the attack and total defeat of pirates, at the island of Basis, Jeba River, Western Africa, Dec. 12, 1849; received the thanks of the lords of the admiralty and was promoted to the brevet rank of major, Feb. 15, 1850; obtained his majority, Sept. 9, 1851; was appointed governor of the settlements, on the Gold Coast, April 1, 1851; was promoted to a lieut.-colonelcy, Feb. 3, 1854; was appointed captain-general and governor-in-chief of Sierra Leone, November 6, 1854; and consul-general in the Sherbro country, on the west coast of Africa, November 11, 1854; which appointment he held till Sept. 1862, when he was appointed gover-
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

nor-in-chief of Antigua, and the Leeward Islands, Nov. 1862; became a colonel, April 6, 1857; exchanged into, and sold out of the 3rd light dragoon; (now hussars) in 1860; his commission of colonel was, Oct. 26, 1858, antedated to Nov. 28, 1854, in accordance with the 14th clause of the royal warrant of Oct. 14, 1858; was acting judge in the mixed commission court at Sierra Leone from Aug. 8, 1858, till Jan. 12, 1859; was appointed C.B., May 18, 1860; governor of Newfoundland, 1869; retired on pension, 1876.

HILL, P. H. R.—Appointed 16th Jan., 1867, clerk and book-keeper to the police establishment, British Guiana; senior inspector of police, 1st July, 1868; acting inspector-general of police from 26th July, 1872, to 26th April, 1873; acting stipendiary magistrate, 14th April 1875.

HIMSWORTH, WILLIAM ALFRED.—Served as clerk in the commissariat, 1838 to 1842; called to the bar in L. C., 1841; clerk in legislative assembly, Canada, 1842-43; appointed clerk in executive council office, 1843, and assistant clerk of the council, 1851; sworn as clerk of the Queen's privy council for Canada, on 1st July, 1872; was secretary to the Confederate Council on Commercial Treaties which sat at Quebec, 1864; is deputy governor for signing money warrants; a commissioner Dedimus Potestatem ; a commissioner in the Queen's Bench for Ontario; and a J.P. for the county of Carleton.

HINCKS, SIR FRANCIS, K.C.M.G. (1869), C.B. (creat. 1862)—Was for several years a prominent member of the Canadian ministry, in which he held the office of inspector-general of accounts; appointed governor-in-chief of Barbados, and the Windward Islands, Oct. 1855; and governor and commander-in-chief, British Guiana, 1861, till commencement of 1869; finance minister for Canada Nov. 1869; resigned February, 1873.
HITCHCOCK, W.—Appointed assistant-refiner in the assay office of British Columbia, Sept. 1857; and since the reduction of the staff in Dec. 1863, has acted as chief melter.

HITCHIN, EDW. WM.—Appointed clerk in the registrar-general's department, South Australia, 1850; clerk in colonial secretary's department, 1851; second assistant colonial secretary, 1856; secretary to commissioner of crown lands and immigration, 1857; secretary to commissioner of public works, 1859; under treasurer, 1860; auditor general, 1868.
HOARE, FREDERICK PUREFOY.—Receiver-general, Trinidad, 1870; collector and receiver of H.M.'s revenue, Gibraltar, Jan., 1872; auditor-general, Malta, 1876.

HOBSON, HENRY PEMBERTON.—Appointed clerk of the peace of Victoria county, Trinidad, Nov. 1860; acting clerk of the peace of Port of Spain, May, 1864; acting clerk of the income tax office, April, 1865; appointed in October, 1867, acting clerk of the peace of Port of Spain; acting stipendiary justice for the western district of the county of St. George, Jan. 1869; stipendiary justice of Oropouche and Moruga wards, June, 1869; is also warden of Moruga, commissioner of crown lands, and commissioner of affidavits.

HOCKING, HENRY HICKS.—Educated at St. John's College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. in 1864 and B.C.L. in 1867; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Nov. 1867, and joined the Home circuit; appointed attorney-general for Western Australia, Dec., 1872.
HODGES, FREDERICK JAMES.—Civil Commissioner and resident magistrate of Robertson division, Cape Colony, appointed Sept., 1871; was
a clerk in the colonial office at the Cape, from August, 1859.

HODGSON, SIR ROBERT, Knt. (creat. 1869).—Admitted to the bar of Nova Scotia, 1819; to the bar of Prince Edward Island, 1819; appointed surrogate and judge of probate, Prince Edward Island, 1828; attorney-general and advocate-general, 1828; appointed to executive and legislative councils, 1829; president of legislative council, 1840; acting chief justice, 1841; resigned office of attorney-general, &c., on introduction of responsible government, 1851; appointed chief justice, 1852; confirmed in the appointment, 1853; judge of court of vice-admiralty, 1853; administered the government during the absence of lieut.-governor Dundas, 1865; lieut.-governor, 1873.

HOLDER, CECIL FREDERIC.—Entered the army as ensign, 62nd foot, May, 1855; lieutenant by purchase, 1855; exchanged to 1st royal dragoons, 1857; captain by purchase, 1859; exchanged to the carabiniers, 1860; transferred to 14th hussars, 1865; served in India and Canada; appointed private secretary and aide-de-camp to the governor of the Leeward Islands, 1873, with rank of lieut.-colonel; clerk to the executive council; appointed private secretary to the governor of Trinidad, 1875.
HOLDSWORTH, E. W. H.—Appointed naturalist to Ceylon government, 1865.

HOLLAND, SIR HENRY THURSTAN, BART., M.P., C.M.G. (1875).—Succeeded to the baronetcy on the death of his father in Oct. 1873. Educated at Harrow and Trinity Coll., Cambridge; was called to the bar of the Inner Temple 16th Nov., 1849; and went the Northern Circuit; was appointed legal adviser to the office of secretary of state for the colonies, January, 1867, and assistant under-secretary of state, March, 1870. Resigned, 3rd August, 1874, and elected member for Midhurst, 22 Sept., 1874; served as a member of the royal commission on the treatment of fugitive slaves, 1876.

HOMAGEE, JAMES FRANCIS.—Joined the judicial establishment of Saint Helena in April, 1859; has been clerk of the peace and registrar of the supreme court since 1867; in 1870 was appointed crown prosecutor and registrar of the vice admiralty court; and in 1875 judge of the summary court and police magistrate.
HORSFALL, J.F.—Officer commanding pioneer division, public works department. Ceylon, 1866; superintending officer, 1869; acting second financial assistant, 1870.

HORSFORD, REV. JOHN, D.D.—Eldest son of the late Hon. John Horsford, M.A. Oxford, for many years president of the legislative council of Antigua, where he temporarily administered the government; entered the civil service as a clerk in the treasury department, where he remained for a short period; in 1837 became a Wesleyan missionary, in which capacity he remained till the year 1869, and from 1848 till 1869 was superintendent of several circuits, comprised in the leeward and windward districts of the West Indies. Is the author of 'A Tour in Europe,' 'A Voice from the West Indies,' 'Philanthropy,' and several ordination charges, delivered by direction of the Wesleyan district meeting. Received in 1861 from the University of Jena the honorary degree of doctor of divinity, in recognition of literary pursuits and of missionary labours in the West Indies; took deacon's orders in June, 1869, and priest's orders in February, 1870, in the Church of England, from the Right Rev. H. H. Parry, D.D., coadjutor bishop; is now island curate of
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
St. John, and also of Christ Church parish, and is licensed by Bishop Parry to perform occasional duty in the parish of Holy Trinity, in the city of Port of Spain, Trinidad.

HOSE, REV. GEORGE FREDERICK, M.A.—Graduated at St. John's College, Cambridge, B.A. 1861, M.A. 1867; curate of Roxton with Great Barford, Beds., 1861-1865; curate of Trinity, St. Marylebone, London, 1865-1867; appointed colonial chaplain of Malacca, November, 1867; colonial chaplain of Singapore, February, 1874; ecclesiastical registrar of the diocese of Labuan, April, 1874.

HOULTON, SIR EDWARD VICTOR LEWIS, M.A., G.C.M.G. (1868), (K.C.M.G., 1859)—Educated at Oriel and St. John's Colleges, Oxford, of which latter college he was elected fellow; graduated B.A. in 1845, M.A. in 1847; was private secretary to Sir Wm. Molesworth, when president of the board of works in 1853, and when secretary of state for the colonies in 1855; appointed chief secretary to the government of Malta, Sept. 1855; is ex officio member of the council of government.
HOWELL, JOHN SIMPSON.—Was employed in the colonial bank at Barbados, and appointed treasurer of that colony, 1860.

HOWLAND, WM. PEARCE, C.B.—Appointed minister of finance for the province of Canada, 24th May, 1862; receiver-general, 16th May, 1863; postmaster-general, Nov. 1864; minister of inland revenue, 1st July, 1867; lieutenant-governor of the province of Ontario, 14th July, 1868, to 5th November, 1873.

HOWSON, W. DANIEL.—Entered the civil service October, 1864. Appointed senior landing waiter in Her Majesty's customs, Sierra Leone, August, 1866; promoted chief clerk of customs, Gold Coast, April, 1867; acting postmaster, Oct. 1870; acting assistant collector of customs, March, 1874.
HOYLES, SIR H. W. Knt. (creat. 1869).—Attorney-general and premier of Newfoundland since the change of government in 1861; appointed chief justice, 1865.
HUBBARD, THOMAS.—First clerk, customs, British Guiana, 1875; entered colonial service, 1861.

HUDSON, GEORGE.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Bathurst division, Cape Colony; and sub-collector of customs, Port Alfred, appointed Jan. 1870; was clerk to R. M., Peddie, in 1864; and in customs department, Port Elizabeth, from 1854 to 1870.
HUDSON, HOUGHAM.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Colesberg division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed June, 1871.

HUDSON, JOHN.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Aliwal (North) division, Cape Colony, appointed Jan., 1869; was clerk at Cradock, 1855 to 1858; ditto in H.M. customs; Port Elizabeth, 1858 to 1859; ditto at Cradock, and clerk of the peace, 1862 to 1864; C. C. and R. M., Victoria W., 1864 to 1869.

HUFFAM, FRED. SOWLEY.—Clerk of the supreme court of Hong Kong, appointed 1859; and registrar of companies, appointed 1865; entered the colonial service, Hong Kong, as 3rd clerk in the colonial secretary's office, in 1855; appointed judge's clerk in 1856; acting deputy registrar, 1860, 1861, 1862; acting registrar, from Nov. 1861, to May, 1862.
HUGESSEN, The Rt. Hon. EDWARD HUGESSEN KNATCHBULL, P.C. (1873).—Son of the late Right Hon. Sir Edward Knatchbull, Bart., who represented East Kent for several years, by his second

wife, Fanny Catherine, daughter of Edward Knight, Esq., of Godmersham Park, and Chawton House, Hants. Born at Mersham Hatch, 1829; married, 1852, Anna Maria Elizabeth, youngest daughter of the Rev. Marens R. Southwell. Educated at Eton and at Magdalen College, Oxford, where he graduated 1850. Assumed the name of Hugessen in addition to his patronymic. Is a magistrate and deputy-lieutenant of Kent, and member of parliament for Sandwich, Deal, and Walmer; was a lord of the treasury from June, 1859, till May, 1866; appointed under secretary of state for the home department, May, 1866; resigned July in same year, and reappointed Dec., 1868; and under-secretary of state for the colonies, Jan. 1871, to Feb. 20, 1874. Author of 'Stories for my Children,' 'Crackers for Christmas,' 'Moonshine,' &c., &c.

HUGGINS, HASTINGS, C., LL.D.—Stipendiary magistrate at British Guiana; called to bar, Inner Temple, Jan. 1858; was Queen's counsel, solicitor-general, and speaker of the house of assembly, and member of the executive council at Nevis; and in 1861 completed a revised compilation of the laws of that island, from 1681 to 1861 inclusive; appointed in 1861 attorney-general of British Honduras, and became ex officio member of the executive council of that colony.

HUGGINS, HORATIO JAMES.—Queen's advocate of Sierra Leone from 9th May, 1863, and chairman of commissioners "for the settlement of claims to land," from 1st Sept. 1863. Is member of the executive and legislative councils; has acted as chief justice on three occasions; became judge ad interim of the mixed courts of justice under the slave trade treaties, on the death of Mr. Skelton, on 21st May, 1865, until the 19th March, 1866; was sent as chief commissioner, with Major Ireland, of the 3rd West India regiment, to the Moriah country, in June, 1865, to effect the release of British subjects seized and imprisoned during the war then raging in that territory between King Boharry and his nephew, Malaghi Baily, and to prevent further injury to life and property; and again as sole commissioner on a second commission to the same country in July, 1865, receiving on both occasions the thanks of the government for the readiness with which he undertook the duties and the "ability he displayed." He also acted as attorney-general in the island of St. Vincent in 1857 and 1858. He was educated at a military college in France, and called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn in Hilary Term, 1838; chief justice, Sierra Leone, 1876.
HULL, HENRY JOCELYN.—Clerk in civil service, Tasmania, August, 1847; finance clerk in lands and works department, February, 1852; deputy commissioner of crown lands, 1st July, 1873.

HULL, HUGH MUNRO.—Clerk to the governor of Tasmania, April, 1834; senior clerk, colonial secretary's office, July, 1841; statistician and secretary to committee of officers, 1844; police magistrate, J.P., coroner, and chairman of quarter sessions, 1856; clerk assistant in parliament, 1857; captain 2nd Volunteer Rifles, 1860; clerk of the house of assembly, April, 1864; was secretary to the reception committee of H.R.H. the Duke of Edinburgh, and also to inter-colonial exhibition commission, and secretary to the Philadelphia exhibition commission; author of "Forty years in Tasmania," "Tasmania in 1871," "Hints to Emigrants," and "Tasmania as a field for British Emigration;" is a fellow of the royal colonial institute and corresponding secretary for Tasmania.

COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
HULL, HUGH —Librarian to the parliamentary library, Tasmania, April, 1867.
HULL, W. WINSTANLEY.—Appointed clerk in the office of the governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands, Barbados, November, 1872; promoted chief clerk, August, 1873.
HULM, V. B.—Appointed clerk in surveyor-general's department at Mauritius, Feb. 1844; promoted to be chief clerk, Jan., 1866.

HUME, W. W.—Appointed acting assistant to the government agent for the western province, Ceylon, 1848; deputy fiscal, and acting assistant agent for the central province, Oct. 1849, 1853; commissioner of requests, Jaffna, in that colony, 1854; temple land commissioner, 1859; senior temple land commissioner, 1860; assistant government agent and commissioner of requests, Matalle, 1865; fiscal, western province, 1867; acting government agent, eastern province, Nov. 1872; appointment confirmed, June, 1873.

HUMPHRYS, WM.—From 20th Aug., 1835, to March, 1837, in H.M.'s customs at Dominica, when he was appointed secretary to the commissioners of compensation; July, 1837, to June, 1838, private secretary to Governor Light, of Dominica; July, 1838, to Jan. 1841, private secretary to Governor Light, British Guiana, and also assistant government secretary; 1843, stipendiary magistrate, and immigration agent general; March, 1853, stipendiary magistrate; sheriff of Essequibo, March, 1863.

HUNT, CAPTAIN JOHN CALVIN.—Half-pay; joined Austrian army, 1844; served in 5th cuirassier regiment; served at siege of Vienna, 1848, and campaign in Hungary; was present at all actions during the campaign; captain, 1852; was with the army of observation on the Vistula and Bukovina, in Poland, 1854; received extra certificates and thanks from the Emperor of Austria for good service and bravery; in 1855 proceeded to England, and joined the cavalry of the British German Legion; in Nov. 1856, proceeded with German Legion to the Cape of Good Hope, where he was employed until disbanded, in 1860; appointed acting deputy-assistant commissary-general at East London, British Kaffraria, 1860; Sept. 1861, appointed acting special magistrate to the Ganobie district, British Kaffraria; Feb. 1870, appointed civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Peddie.
HUNT, R. F.—Was appointed clerk to the colonial secretary of Bermuda, Sept. 10th, 1875.
HUNTER, A.—Public medical officer, British Honduras.

HUXTABLE, HENRY ANTHONY.—Has been in the office of the procureur-general of Mauritius since the 1st of February, 1868; appointed in June, 1872, a clerk to the royal commissioners of inquiry into the treatment of immigrants in Mauritius.

HYDE, CHARLES TUNSTALL.—Was private secretary and aide-de-camp to Governor Hill at Antigua in 1863 was appointed inspector-general of police in that island, March, 1867, and auditor-general of the colony in May, 1867; administered the government of the Virgin Islands from July to end of Oct. 1869; appointed provost-marshal of Barbados, 1870.

IBBOTT, JOHN THOMAS.—Sub-controller of customs and sub-bookkeeper, Berbice, 16th March, 1875; acting tide waiter, Georgetown, Demerara, from the 9th July, 1869, to the 1st of Feb., 1860; clerk in charge of the colonial bonded warehouse, Georgetown, 1st Feb., 1860; acting sub-controller of customs and sub-bookkeeper, Berbice, from the 9th Sept., 1869, to the 23rd December, 1870; acting assistant government secretary, and acting re-

ceiver-general, Berbice, from the 24th Dec., 1870, to the 23rd March, 1872; appointed on the 16th Feb., 1870, 1st clerk to the customs, Georgetown, Demerara; assumed the duties on the 23rd March, 1872; acted as comptroller of customs in 1872, 1874, and 1876.
IMLACH, ROBERT WIGHT.—Crown solicitor, British Guiana.

INGLOTT, FERDINANDO V., C.M.G.—Was appointed a teacher of a government primary school at Malta, Nov. 1841; served in the naval department from 1843 to 1855; assistant superintendent of ports, 1858; comptroller of charitable institutions, Jan. 1859; is ex officio member of council.

INNES, SIR JOSEPH GEORGE LONG, KNIGHT BACHELOR (creat. 1875.)—Eldest son of Captain Joseph Long Innes, late H.M. 39th regiment of foot. Born at Sydney, N.S.W., October 16th, 1834. Educated at the King's School, Paramatta. Called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, Michaelmas term, 1859 (certificate of honour, Trinity term, 1859). Admitted to bar of New South Wales, February, 1862. Appointed district judge in Queensland, December, 1865; resigned that office and returned to practice at bar of N. S. W., July, 1869; elected member of legislative assembly, March, 1872; solicitor-general of N. S. W., 14th May, 1872; attorney-general 20th November, 1873, to 9th February, 1875. In Aug., 1872, was called to the upper house of parliament, in N. S. W., in which chamber he represented the government. In Sept.-Oct., 1874, accompanied Sir Hercules Robinson on his special mission in connection with the annexation of Fiji. For his services on that occasion received the honour of knighthood.

INNES, J. R.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Bedford division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed Oct. 1867; also inspector of roads; was teacher in government schools, 1842; clerk to superintendent-general of education, 1845; clerk in colonial secretary's office, 1847 to 1856; magistrate of Riversdale, 1856-1863; ditto of Urtenhage, 1863 to 1857.

INNISS, WILLIAM E.—Joined the civil service at Sierra Leone in the surveyor's department, December, 1866, as artizan till June, 1871; held the appointment of under goaler, June, 1871, to December, 1872, then transferred to the harbour works as artizan; appointed keeper of the gaol, August, 1874.

IRVING, CHARLES J.—Was in the colonial land and emigration office from June 1852 to Nov. 1853; clerk in the audit office, Somerset House, from Nov. 1853 to July 1864, when he was appointed special clerk, audit office, Mauritius; appointed auditor-general of the Straits Settlements and member of the executive and legislative councils of the colony, 1st April, 1867.

IRVING, EDWARD.—Was in the colonial land and emigration office from April, 1852, until 1st January, 1855, when he was appointed private secretary to Sir F. Elliot, assistant under-secretary of state for the colonies; appointed to establishment of the office, 1860; 1st junior class, 1st Oct. 1866; private secretary to Sir F. Sandford, December, 1868; assistant colonial secretary, Straits Settlements, 20th November, 1869; acting auditor-general, January to July, 1870, and again from March, 1874, to the end of the year.

IRVING, HENRY T., C.M.G. (1874).—Appointed clerk in colonial office, November, 1854; promoted to 3rd class, October, 1859; appointed private secretary to Sir F. Rogers, now Lord Blachford, under-secretary of state, 1862; promoted to 2nd class, April, 1863; appointed colonial secretary of
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
Jamaica, June, 1866; colonial secretary, Ceylon, May, 1869, and Governor of the Leeward Islands, 1873; Governor of Trinidad, July, 1874.

ISEMONGER, EDWIN EMPSON.—Appointed to the audit office, London, 7th February, 1856; chief clerk to auditor-general, Straits Settlements, 4th Sept. 1867; was acting colonial treasurer, commissioner of stamps, accountant-general to the supreme court, and member of both councils, Straits Settlements, from 3rd March, 1869, to 10th May, 1870; appointed assistant treasurer, Straits Settlements, Nov. 1869; is justice of the peace for the Settlements; placed in charge of Malacca treasury and stamp offices, September, 1872; duties of collector of land revenue added, February, 1874; appointed magistrate of police and commissioner of court of requests, Malacca, 1st June, 1874.

ISNARD, L.—Clerk in registrar's department, Mauritius, May, 1854; clerk to the second puisne judge of supreme court of that island, April, 1859; clerk to first puisne judge, 1863; appointed chief clerk to the registrar, 1st March, 1864.
JACKSON, CHARLES HAMILTON.—Appointed island secretary for Jamaica, and official member of the legislative council, 1873; island secretary and administrator general.

JACKSON, FRANCIS EDWARD.—M.B., University of Aberdeen; member of the royal college of surgeons of England; member and licentiate of the apothecaries' society of London; certificated by the Privy Council as a public vaccinator; studied at Saint Bartholomew's Hospital, the London Hospital, and University of Aberdeen; colonial surgeon, province Wellesley, Penang, 1874; magistrate and J.P. for the Settlement of Penang; superintendent of vaccination for province Wellesley.

JACKSON, MAXIMILIAN JAMES.—Special magistrate and inspector under Northern Border Protection Act, appointed October, 1868, Cape of Good Hope; was clerk at Victoria (East), 1858 to 1864; ditto at Richmond, 1864 to 1865; acting C. C. and R. M. Richmond, for four months during this period; clerk at Murraysburg, 1865 to 1868; C. C. and R. M., Richmond, 1873.
JACKSON, HARRY M., LIEUT., R.N.—Appointed A.D.C. to governor of Trinidad, Sept., 1874; private secretary, Nov., 1874.
JACKSON, THOMAS W.—Appointed magistrate at the Gambia, 1869; a puisne judge, Gold Coast, 1876.

JACOBS, SIMEON.—Is attorney-general, Cape of Good Hope, appointed, 1874; obtained a licence from the Hon. Society of the Inner Temple to practise as a special pleader, November, 1851; called to the bar, 17th Nov., 1852; admitted as an advocate of the supreme court, Cape of Good Hope, Dec., 1860; appointed attorney-general of British Kaffraria, 4th April, 1861; solicitor-general, Cape of Good Hope, 1866.

JAMES, JOHN CHARLES HORSEY.—Born at Rome, 1841; educated at Rugby and at Exeter College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A., 1864; called to the bar by the Inner Temple, Nov., 1866, and is a member of the Oxford circuit; appointed commissioner of titles in Western Australia, July 9th, 1875.

JANISCH, H. R.—Appointed police magistrate, St. Helena, from 1851; was acting queen's advocate 1856, 1857; and acting colonial secretary, 1861 and 1868; has been in colonial service since 1838; was colonial secretary and auditor-general; Governor of St. Helena, 1873.
JAUNCEY, FRANCIS HASTINGS.—Appointed 5th Nov., 1871; cashier in Treasury, Lagos, 1st January, 1872, private secretary to Sir John
Hawley Glover, G.C.M.G., and clerk of council same colony, 15th July, 1875; appointed Registrar of Titles, Western Australia, under the Transfer Land Act, 1874.

JEANS, JOSEPH HENRY.—Formerly colonial surgeon, Bathurst, Gambia, appointed Oct., 1867; licentiate royal college of physicians; member royal college of surgeons; licentiate apothecaries' company; staff assistant surgeon, Canterbury; surgeon to H.M.S. 'Adriatic;' hon. surgeon to 2nd Hampshire Light Horse; coroner; officer of health; quarantine officer of health; magistrate; commissioner of the court of requests for the above settlement. Commoner, Downing College, Cambridge; and Pensioner at Christ's College; now assistant surgeon at Lagos.

JEKYLL, HERBERT.—Received commission as lieutenant in royal engineers, 17th April, 1866; employed under War Office and Trinity House in submarine mining and destruction of sunken ships, from 1868 to 1870, and under G.P.O. in the telegraph department, from 1870 to 1873, and 1874 to 1876; served in the campaign of 1873-74 on the Gold Coast under Sir Garnet Wolseley; appointed private secretary to the Earl of Carnarvon, January, 1877.
JENKINS, J. W.—Colonial surveyor, superintendent of public works, commissioner of roads, &c., Sierra Leone, May, 1868.

JENNINGS, MATTHEW.—Sub-collector of customs, Port Elizabeth, appointed 1st July, 1870; appointed clerk in the customs departments, Cape Town, Sept., 1848; acting sub-collector at Port Beaufort, 1850; sub-collector, East London, 1851; controller of customs and navigation laws, 1852; shipping-master and justice of the peace for the port of East London, 1855; resident magistrate of the district of East London, 1857; immigration agent, 1858; collector of customs for British Kaffraria, 1859; civil commissioner, East London, Cape of Good Hope.

JERVOIS, COLONEL SIR WILLIAM FRANCIS DRUMMOND, K.C.M.G. (1874), C.B., R.E.—Served at the Cape of Good Hope from April, 1841, until September, 1848, of which time he was six years on the frontier and in Kaffraria. In 1842 he was appointed to the staff as brigade-major to a force of all arms sent on an expedition against the Dutch Boers; served during the Kaffir war of 1846-7, accompanying Sir Henry Pottinger and Lieut.-general Sir George Berkeley to the scene of hostilities; was engaged during the war in active operations generally, and in making a military map of Kaffraria, which was subsequently engraved and published; in 1845-6 visited Natal, and returning overland to the Cape Colony, he laid down a map of the country between the two colonies. During the six years he served on the frontier, he was also engaged in making surveys, in constructing roads, building bridges, and improving the communications of the country. From 1852 to 1855 was specially employed at Alderney in designing and executing the fortifications of that island. From 1856 to 1875 was on the staff of the war office, first as assistant inspector-general of fortifications, and subsequently as deputy-director of fortifications. From 1857 to 1875 was secretary of the committee on the defence of the empire, and in 1859 he was secretary to the royal commission on national defences. During the time he was in the war office he was employed in the design and execution of, and on the business connected with the whole of the extensive works of fortifications now nearly completed in this country, and in the British foreign possessions; in 1863 was nominated
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

a civil C.B.; in 1863 was sent to Canada, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia and Bermuda, to report on the defences of those provinces; and again in 1864 to confer with the Canadian government as to the defences of the dominion. His proposals were approved by the imperial and colonial authorities, and those for Bermuda and Quebec have been adopted and carried out; in 1865 was sent to Malta, and in 1866 to Gibraltar, to prepare projects for the improvement of the defences at those fortresses. He again visited Halifax and Bermuda in 1869, and made further reports on the defences; in 1871-2 was employed by the government of India to inspect and report upon the defences of Aden, Perim, Bombay, and the Hooghly; and submitted reports to the secretary of state for India in council respecting the defences of those places. By desire of the viceroy of India, and with the approval of the secretary of state for India, he also visited British Burmah, and subsequently submitted reports with regard to the defences of Rangoon and Moulmein. Appointed to the order of St. Michael and St. George for his colonial services; in April, 1875, appointed governor of the Straits Settlements.
JEVERS, R. W.—Educated at the Queen's University in Ireland, where he graduated in classics with honours; appointed writer in the Ceylon civil service, April, 1872.
JEWELL, R. R.—Clerk of the works in Western Australia; appointed 1853.
JOHNSON, SAMUEL OTIS.—Member of the Executive Council, Bahamas, 1875.

JOHNSON, THOMAS.—Appointed clerk to the government steamer 'Gambia,' 1847; third writer in the colonial secretary's office, Gambia, 1852; colonial registrar, 1855; registrar of deeds, 7th June, 1858, lieutenant of the British Combo militia, 12th April, 1862; served as second writer, 1852; and as postmaster, 1859 and 1860, and again in 1869; promoted second writer, and collector of rates for local improvements, Dec., 1862; acting as colonial engineer from Aug., 1866, to Dec., 1868; promoted to the office of first clerk and cashier in the collector and treasurer's office, Jan. 1, 1869.
JOHNSON, D. S.—Appointed dispenser in hospital at Sierra Leone, 17th Dec., 1858; promoted sanitary inspector, February, 1857.

JOHNSTON, W. W. W.—Lieutenant-colonel; entered the army, 1854; as a subaltern frequently employed on the staff in the West Indies and on the West Coast of Africa; promoted captain, 1862; commanded the troops in Honduras in 1864; aide-de-camp to major-general O'Connor, C.B., commanding the forces in Jamaica, &c., 1865; at the outbreak of the Jamaica disturbances same year, appointed deputy adjutant-general of volunteers, and as such served under the following governors: Mr. Eyre, Sir Henry Storks, and Sir J. P. Grant; appointed, by Sir H. Storks, commissioner to investigate and report on claims in connection with the late rebellion, to March, 1867; promoted to the rank of major, in the 1st West India regiment, 20th July, 1867; commanded the troops in the Gambia, 1868, 1869, during which time assisted the chief magistrate as a justice of the peace and commissioner of the court of requests; also acted as coroner; commanded the troops in British Honduras, and was a member of the executive and legislative councils, from 1871 to end of 1873; employed on the Frontier, on the occasion of the invasion of the Northern District by the Ycaiche Indians of Yucatan, 1872; received the thanks of the lieutenant-governor for this service; also received the thanks of the inhabitants in a memorial sent through the

governor, and on departure from British Honduras to join in the Ashanti expedition, was presented with a farewell address from the inhabitants of that colony; commanded the head-quarters of the 1st West India regiment during its advance across the Prah, with the advanced forces under major-general Sir Garnet Wolseley; promoted lieutenant-colonel, 24th December, 1873; on the withdrawal of the forces from the Gold Coast, succeeded to the command of the troops on the West Coast of Africa, and as such administered the government of the Gold Coast for a short time; member of the executive and legislative council of the Settlement of Sierra Leone.
JOHORE, MAHARAJAH OF.—His Highness Sir Datu Tummongong Abubakr Sri, K.C.S.I.; G.C.M.G. (1876).
JOLLIVET, ISIDORE.—District magistrate, Mauritius, February, 1862.
JONES, P.—Assistant treasurer, collector of stamps, and accountant-general supreme court, Penang; appointed clerk in treasury, March, 1851; promoted to be assistant treasurer, 1867.

JOSEPH, SIDNEY.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, July, 1843; was private secretary to Rt. Hon. Sir Jas. Stephen for seven years, and subsequently acted in the same capacity to Mr. Merivale for ten years; promoted assistant clerk, November, 1858; first class clerk, 30th September, 1872: Parliamentary clerk of the colonial office.

JUDGE, EDWARD ARTHUR.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Queenstown division, Cape Colony; appointed December, 1858; was magistrate's clerk, Simon's Town, 1856 to 1857; clerk in Colonial Office, 1857 to 1861; acting C. C. and R. M., Namaqualand, 1861 to 1864; C. C. and R. M., Namaqualand, 1864 to 1869; inspector-general of chests, &c., 1875.

JULYAN, SIR PENROSE G., K.C.M.G. (1874), C.B. (1869).—Served as a volunteer during the Canadian rebellion in 1837-8; special commissioner of roads and bridges in Lower Canada in 1839; appointed to the commissariat in 1845. During the great immigration into Canada, which followed the Irish famine in 1847, was selected by the commander of the forces to organize, for the colony, an extensive quarantine establishment on Grosse Isle, in the river St. Lawrence, with a view of checking the terrible epidemic brought to that country by the emigrants. For the successful manner in which this service was performed, he was promoted by the Lords Commissioners of Her Majesty's Treasury to the rank of deputy-assistant commissary-general, and received the thanks of the colonial government. Was appointed assistant financial secretary to the board of works in Ireland in 1848; nominated director of a branch of the royal mint in Australia in 1852, and was employed on mint service until the war broke out with Russia, and was then recalled to the commissariat; was employed as an engineer during the early part of the war, and then designed and superintended the construction of a "Marine Steam Flour Mill" and a "Floating Bakery" for the supply of the army in the Crimea, which rendered valuable services throughout the war. For these and other inventions, which led to great economy and efficiency in army administration, received the special thanks of the Minister for War, and was promoted to the rank of assistant commissary-general, January 2, 1856; and created a C.B. in May, 1869. While in the commissariat, served in Great Britain, Ireland, North America, and the Mediterranean; was appointed crown agent for the colonies in 1858; sent on a special commission of
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
inquiry into the civil establishments and general expenditure of Mauritius, 1873.
JUMEAUX, ARTHUR.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1865; attached to Jaffna Katcheri, Feb. 1868; commissioner of requests, &c., Avisavila and Pasyala, 1871; acting police magistrate, Matara, May, 1873.

KEENS, JAMES HENRY.—Formerly president of the privy council; assistant justice of the court of common pleas; member of the legislative council, 1845; and a member of the executive committee, Tobago; was master in chancery from 1837 to 1853; police magistrate from 1838 to 1856; administered government of Tobago for ten months in 1856 and 1857.
KEITH, W. G.—Appointed assistant colonial surgeon to government of Ceylon, 1870.
KELLY, L. F.—Appointed deputy assistant commissary-general at Trincomalee, Ceylon, 1862; appointed writer, Ceylon civil service, 1868, and attached to Colombo Katcheri.
KELLY, REV. WM. FRED., M.A.—Was colonial chaplain of Matelle, Ceylon; appointed by the Bishop of Colombo to the chaplaincy of Nuwara Eliya, 1864; colonial chaplain, St. Peter's church, 1871.

KELLY, RIGHT REV. JAMES B.—Educated at Clare College, Cambridge, B.A., 1854, M.A. 1858, and D.D. 1867; was formerly vicar of Kirk-Michael, Isle of Man, and chaplain to the Bishop of Sodor and Man; appointed arch-deacon of Newfoundland and Labrador, 1865; consecrated coadjutor bishop of Newfoundland, 1867.

KENDALL, CAPT. J. J.—Entered Her Majesty's 44th regiment as ensign, 28th August, 1854; appointed lieutenant, 9th March, 1855; captain, 31st December, 1858; served with the 44th regiment; served in the Crimea from 1st June, 1855, including the siege and fall of Sebastopol, and attacks on the Redan on the 8th and 18th of June (medal and clasps, and Turkish medal); served with a wing of the 1st battalion, 6th regiment, throughout the operations against the Hill tribes in Sikkim in 1861, and was in temporary command of the field force employed in that expedition during the absence of Lieut.-Col. Gawler, the brigadier; also held a command in the army of the late Emperor Maximilian in Mexico, from the year 1865, until the death of His Majesty, by whom he was decorated with the cross of "Guadaloupe" for distinguished service in the field at the battle of "Las Cruces," on which occasion he had two horses shot under him; appointed colonial secretary of Sierra Leone, in Sept. 1868; appointed administrator-in-chief of the West Africa settlements by a commission, dated 22nd April, 1869, and administered the government-in-chief from 15th Aug. to 8th Dec. 1869; administered the government of Sierra Leone on several subsequent occasions during the temporary absence of the governor; acted as police magistrate of Sierra Leone from 8th Aug. 1870 to April, 1871; again administered the government-in-chief during portions of the months of May and June, 1871.

KENNEDY, KT. BACHEL. (1867), K.C.M.G. (1871), C.B. (1862), SIR ARTHUR EDWARD.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin. Entered the army as ensign in the 11th regiment in 1827; lieutenant 1832; captain 68th light infantry, 1840; retired from the army in 1848 to accept civil employment; was selected (while serving in the army) in 1846 to fill the office of county inspector under the board of works; and served uninterruptedly during the Irish famine, as inspector under Sir John Burgoyne's relief commissioner; and sub-

sequently as inspector of poor laws, till the office was abolished in 1851; appointed governor of the "Gambia," and transferred to Sierra Leone in 1852; consul-general of the adjoining Sherbro County, in 1854; and removed in the same year to the government of Western Australia; administered the latter government without interruption till February, 1862; created Companion of the Bath in 1862; appointed governor of Vancouver's Island, November, 1863; governor of the West Africa Settlements, November, 1867; and governor of Hong Kong, 1872; governor of Queensland, 1877.

KENNY, KT. BACHEL. (Created 1870), SIR EDWARD KENNY.—Born 1800; sat in legislative council of Nova Scotia for 26 years, during 12 of which he was president of that body; called to the Senate, 1867; receiver-general, July, 1867; president of privy council, Oct. 1869; administrator of the government of Nova Scotia for several months in 1870.

KER, ALAN.—Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, Nov. 1842; chief justice of Nevis, 1854; appointed chief justice of Dominica, 1856; and assistant judge of the supreme court of Jamaica, 1861; acted as attorney-general of Antigua, from August, 1851, to March, 1854; member of legislative council, May, 1876.

KERR, THOMAS.—Was employed in the colonial secretary's department, Barbados, in 1856. Appointed police magistrate for the parish of St. Andrew, April, 1860; promoted to the parish of Christchurch, October, 1861, and to the city of Bridgetown, June, 1866; judge of the petty debt court of Bridgetown, Sept. 1869. Major of the 11th or St. Joseph's regiment militia, 1854; commanding officer since 1858. Represented the parish of St. Joseph in the house of assembly from 1862 to 1866. Member of council 1876.
KEYT, F.—Licentiate of medicine, Bengal College; medical assistant, Ceylon, 1863; assistant colonial surgeon, 1867.

KIDD, RICHARD HAYWARD, M.A.—Educated privately, and at Christ's College, Cambridge; deacon, 1862; priest, 1863, by Bishop of Norwich; curate of All Saints with St. Pileau, Norwich, 1862, 1863; incumbent of St. Benedict's, Norwich, 1863—1865; secretary of the Irish Society, 1866, 1867; rector of St. Michael Coslany, Norwich, 1867—1871; appointed colonial chaplain, Hong Kong, May, 1871; author of various published sermons, reports, and tracts.

KILLIKELLY, WM. EDW.—Educated for the army at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst. Was private secretary and aide-de-camp to his father during his administration of the government of St. Kitts in 1833; entered the service of the imperial customs at Demerara in 1835; promoted in same department to St. Kitts in 1836; where he continued to serve until 1844, when he was promoted to be comptroller and landing surveyor at Grenada; specially appointed to be acting collector at Grenada in 1845, and comptroller of navigation laws in 1849; appointed comptroller of colonial customs in 1855; was private secretary to Lieut.-Governor Kortright from June, 1859, to Jan. 1864; held the same appointment under Lieut.-Governor Mundy; is a justice of the peace, a member of assembly; aide-de-camp to the lieut.-governor; appointed police magistrate of the northern district, Grenada, Aug. 1865.
KIMBERLEY, 1ST EARL OF (United Kingdom), created 1866; BARON WODEHOUSE (Great Britain), 1797; baronet, 1811; privy councillor, 1864.—JOHN WODEHOUSE, son of the late Hon. Henry Wode-
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

house (who died in 1834) by the only daughter of Theophilus Thomhagh Gurdon, Esq., of Letton, Norfolk; born in London, 1826; married, 1847, the eldest daughter of the 3rd Earl of Clare; succeeded his grandfather in 1846; educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1847; was appointed a deputy-lieutenant of Norfolk, 1847; was under-secretary for foreign affairs from Dec. 1852, to April, 1856; envoy extraordinary and minister plenipotentiary to Russia from May, 1856, to March, 1858; again under-secretary for foreign affairs from June, 1859, to July, 1861; under-secretary of state for India, 1864; was lord privy seal, Dec. 1868, to July, 1870; secretary of state for the colonies, 6th July, 1870, to 20th Feb. 1874; has been a member of the senate of the University of London since 1859; sent on a special mission to Copenhagen, December, 1863; lord-lieutenant of Ireland from Oct. 1864, to June, 1866, when he was created an earl; is patron of eleven livings. The first baronet in this family had previously been knighted for his conduct at the siege of Cadiz; the 5th baronet represented Norfolk in five parliaments, and the 6th was the 1st Baron Wodehouse, and great grandfather of the present earl.

KING, CHARLES KEASLEY BERRY. — Educated at Stonyhurst College; matriculated at the London University, 1855; appointed 1st clerk and 3rd sub-immigration agent, British Guiana, 1st November, 1871; 2nd sub-immigration agent, 6th June, 1872; sub-immigration agent in charge of Berbice District, 1st January, 1875.

KING, Æ. A. — Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, 1865, and in the same year acting police magistrate of Upper and Lower Doombera; acting assistant government agent, Kandy, 1866; commissioner of requests, &c., Harrispattu, 1866; acting assistant government agent, Nuwara Eliya, 1867; acting police magistrate, Gampola, Dec. 1867; district judge, &c., Badulla, 1868; acting assistant government agent, &c., Hambantota, 1870; district judge, Kegalla, Sept. 1872; assistant government agent, Kegalla, June, 1873; acting postmaster-general, Oct. 1873.

KING, F. EUSTACE. — Clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Barbados, from May, 1866, to May, 1868; transferred from there to the government secretary's office, British Guiana, in June, 1868, and served there as clerk until December, 1870; inspector of police and justice of the peace, British Guiana, January, 1871; commissary of taxation, July, 1873, until 25th June, 1875, when he was appointed emigration agent at Barbados for British Guiana.

KING, FRANCIS OGLEBY. — Registrar of the county of Berbice, British Guiana; entered registrar's office of Demerara and Essequibo, British Guiana, as a clerk and copyist, on 1st April, 1858; appointed third assistant sworn clerk in said office on 28th January, 1860; second assistant sworn clerk on 21st July, 1860; and first assistant sworn clerk in June, 1862; acted as sworn clerk and notary public from 8th October, 1863, to 7th April, 1864; and again from 6th June to 29th August, 1867; appointed sworn clerk and notary public 12th February, 1868, after examination before the judges of the Supreme Court of Civil Justice of said colony; registrar of the county of Berbice, 7th July, 1868; justice of peace, and also commissioner for administering oaths to affidavits, 18th July, 1868; acted as sub-comptroller of customs and sub-comptroller of duties, and sub-book-keeper for the said county of Berbice from 1st of August to 31st October, 1872; special justice of peace, 11th June, 1875.
KING, H. — Appointed acting commissary taxa-
tion, British Guiana, 1st July, 1865, to 30th June, 1866; magistrates' clerk, 1st August, 1867.
KING, H. G. — Clerk to auditor-general; clerk to commissioners for rebuilding the town of Basse-terre, St. Kitts; provincial treasurer; appointed cashier and bookkeeper treasury department, 1873.

KING, J. HAMPDEN. — Graduated at Oxford; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, May, 1843; was a member of the house of assembly in Barbados from 1847 to 1853, and again in 1862; appointed a judge of the court of appeal at Barbados, 1853; acted as colonial secretary from April, 1860, to March, 1862; acted as auditor-general from March to May, 1862; appointed to administer the government of St. Kitts during the absence of Sir B. C. C. Pine, 6th August, 1862; second puisne judge, British Guiana, 1868. Acting chief justice, 1875-76.
KING, T. N. — Appointed assistant commissary of taxation, British Guiana, 23rd June, 1874.

KINGSTON (JAMAICA), 1st BISHOP of. Founded 1856. — RIGHT REV. REGINALD COURTENAY, D.D. — Educated at Magdalen Hall, Oxford, where he graduated B.A., 1835, M.A. 1838; appointed archdeacon of Middlesex in Jamaica in 1853; and coadjutor to the Bishop of Jamaica, under the title of Bishop of Kingston, Feb. 1856; a privy councillor in Jamaica, Sept. 1856.

KIRKE, HENRY. — Graduated at Oxford B.A. 1863, M.A. 1866, B.C.L. 1866. Called to the bar, Inner Temple, January, 1868; went the Midland circuit; Derby and Birmingham sessions; appointed stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana, Sept. 1872; acting puisne judge in the same colony, April 7th, 1874.

KIRKWALL, VISCOUNT, K.C.M.G. (1875), (C.M.G. 1864). — Entered the army as an ensign in the 92nd Highlanders, Aug. 8, 1845; lieutenant, Nov. 9, 1846; captain, Nov. 1853; exchanged into the 71st Highland light infantry in 1854; served with that regiment in the trenches before Sebastopol; was at the taking of Kertch and Yanakale; received a medal and clasp for Sebastopol, and Turkish medal was granted March 2, 1858; Her Majesty's royal license and permission to accept and wear the insignia of the 5th class of the imperial order of the Medjidie, which his Imperial Majesty the Sultan was pleased to confer upon him, as a mark of his majesty's approbation of his distinguished services before the enemy in the late war. Served as aide-de-camp to Sir Henry Ward, G.C.M.G., then lord high commissioner in the Ionian Islands, from 1851 to 1854; appointed to the Scots Fusilier Guards in 1856; sold out of the army in 1857; is a deputy-lieutenant of Ayrshire, and a captain in the royal Bucks yeomanry cavalry; received the grand cross of St. Saviour from the king of the Hellenes, July, 1868.

KNIGHT, ARTHUR. — Appointed short-hand reporter to the legislative council of the Straits Settlements, 1st April, 1869; acting chief clerk, audit office, 6th September, 1869; chief clerk, same department, 21st October, 1873. Accompanied Governor Sir Harry Ord on his special mission to Labuan in 1873, and assisted in the investigation of the financial and commercial condition of that colony; acting assistant colonial secretary and clerk of councils, 14th May, 1875.

KNOLLYS, C. C. — Educated at Magdalen College, Oxford, where he gained an open scholarship in 1868; 1st class in mathematical moderations, 1870; 2nd class in the final schools, 1872; B.A., 1873; appointed sub-receiver and harbour master at San Fernando, Trinidad, 1874.
KOCH, REV. C. A. — Appointed colonial chaplain, Jaffna, Ceylon, 1869.
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
KOCH, E. L.—Licentiate of medicine and surgeon, Bengal college; appointed medical assistant to Ceylon government, 1862; assistant colonial surgeon 1st class, 1867.

KORTRIGHT, CORNELIUS H.—Appointed a local magistrate at the Bahamas, 19 Feb., 1849; president administering the government of the Virgin Islands, 1854; lieut.-governor of Grenada, Nov. 1856; appointed to be lieut.-governor of Tobago, April, 1864; was acting governor, Trinidad, in 1868; appointed administrator of the government of the Gambia, 1873; governor of the West Africa Settlements, 1875; governor of British Guiana, 1877.
KRIEKENBEEK, C. A., M. D. — Appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon; 3rd class, 1843; 2nd class, 1845; 1st class, 1854; assistant colonial surgeon, 1858; ditto, 1st class, 1867.
KRIEKENBEEK, C. I., M.B.C.M.—Appointed assistant colonial surgeon in the medical department, Ceylon, April, 1869.
KYNNERSLEY, C. W. SNEYD.—Appointed to the civil service, Straits Settlements, 1872; secretary assistant lieut.-governor's office, Penang, 1873.

KYSHE, J. B., F.S.S.—Assistant district clerk, Mauritius, 10th August, 1857; clerk, colonial secretary's office, 15th August, 1859; acting registrar, 15th March, 1860; financial and statistical clerk, 10th December, 1860; financial clerk and president of the stamping committee, 1st September, 1866; government translator, 22nd November, 1866; secretary, civil service enquiry commission, 4th December, 1868; secretary, Duke of Edinburgh's reception committee, 14th December, 1869; secretary, census commission, 12th May, 1870; secretary, census commission, 1st February, 1871; acting registrar-general, 4th June, 1872; acting secretary to councils of government, 14th July, 1873; financial and statistical clerk, C.S.O., 25th November, 1873 acting; secretary to councils of government 1st October, 1874.

LABASTIDE, LOUIS AUGUSTUS DE JACQUES DE, C.E.—Entered the public service at Trinidad on the 1st of July, 1869, in the office of public works; was appointed chief draughtsman on 1st January, 1870; graduated at the University of Paris; is a bachelor of science; gained the prize in high mathematics at St. Louis College, Paris; was then admitted a member of "special mathematics" society in that college; was admitted into the government engineering school "Ecole Impériale Centrale des Arts et Manufactures;" was admitted a sworn land surveyor in March, 1872.

LABORDE, EDWARD.—Colonial secretary and registrar-general, St. Vincent, appointed 1861; entered the colonial service as clerk in the treasury of that island, 1850; deputy treasurer, 1852; acting colonial secretary, 1856; police magistrate, 1857; stipendiary magistrate and coroner, 1858; provost-marshal, 1860; commanded, until 1868, the St. George's volunteers, which were raised by him in 1863; administered the government of Grenada in 1871; and that of St. Vincent in 1872; administered the government of Grenada again in 1874.

LABORDE, Rev. H. W.—Rector of St. George's and St. Andrew's parishes, and chaplain of the gaol, St. Vincent, appointed 1852; M.A. Cambridge, where he graduated B.A., 1845; was ordained assistant curate in St. Vincent, 1845; was minister of All Saints' chapel and garrison, chaplain of the island of Trinidad, 1850 to 1852; is chaplain to the Bishop of Barbados; was appointed the rural dean of St. Vincent, 1864; and also a member of the legislative council.
LABUAN, 2nd BISHOP of. Consecrated, July 29th, 1869.—RIGHT REV. DR. CHAMBERS.—For many years archdeacon of Sarawak.

LAIRD, DAVID.—Born 1833; sat in Prince Edward Island Assembly from 1871 to 1873, when returned to the dominion house of commons, where he still sits. Was a member of the executive council, P. E. I., from Nov. 1872, to April, 1873, and whilst holding that position negotiated for the entrance of the colony into the dominion; sworn of the privy council, and appointed minister of the interior of the dominion, 7th Nov., 1873; lieutenant-governor of the north-west territories, 7th Oct., 1876.
LAKE, ALEXANDER.—A solicitor in Jamaica; clerk of the peace for St. Mary's, and clerk of the St. Ann's district court, Jamaica.
LAMOND, G.—Appointed clerk in the audit office, Natal, 1853; and second clerk in the office of colonial secretary of that colony, 1859; 1st clerk, 1864.
LAMOTHE, CHAS.—Appointed inspector of distilleries at Mauritius, Jan. 1856; acting superintendent of same, September, 1862; superintendent, July, 1863.
LANDOR, E. W.—Appointed police magistrate, Perth, W. Australia, 1865.
LANE, COLONEL CECIL NEWTON, C.M.G. (1864).—Was a resident at Cephalonia until the cession of the Ionian Islands to the Kingdom of Greece.
LANG, WM. M.D.—Health officer, Carriacou, Grenada, 1863; medical officer, St. Andrew's parish, 1867.

LANGEVIN, EDOUARD JOSEPH, N.P.—Admitted as a notary public, Lower Canada, 3rd Dec., 1858; a member of board of notaries of Lower Canada for several years; appointed clerk of the Crown in Chancery, Canada, 4th Jan., 1865; to same office for dominion, 5th July, 1867; deputy registrar-general, 1st July, 1868; under secretary of state of Canada, 9th July, 1873; was major 9th Batt. V.M., or Voltigeurs de Québec, and retired retaining rank, 1865.

LANGEVIN, HECTOR LOUIS, C.B. (1868), Q.C. —Born, 1826; called to the bar of Lower Canada, 1850; created a queen's counsel, 1864; mayor of Quebec from 1858 to 1861, and as such received H.R.H. the Prince of Wales on his landing in Canada, 1860; entered Canadian parliament, 1857, and has almost uninterruptedly held a seat up to the present time, first in the old legislative assembly, and since confederation, in the house of commons of the dominion; appointed a member of the executive council, Canada, and solicitor-general for Lower Canada, 30th March, 1864; postmaster-general, Nov., 1866; at confederation, 1st July, 1867, sworn of the queen's privy council and appointed secretary of state of Canada; minister of public works, 8th Dec., 1869, and whilst holding that office was elected as the successor of the late Sir G. E. Cartier, Bart., as leader of the liberal conservative party of Lower Canada; resigned office, 5th Nov., 1873; was one of the framers of the present Canadian constitution, and in furtherance of the cause of confederation attended the colonial conferences at Charlottetown and Quebec in 1864, and in London in 1866, when the Act of Union was carried through the imperial parliament; created a Knight Commander of the Roman Order of St. Gregory the Great, 1870; is the author of a prize essay on Canada, and of several other works.
LANGTON, JOHN, M.A.—Sat in Canadian parliament from 1851 to 1855, when appointed auditor
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

of public accounts for province; appointed auditor of the dominion, 30th May, 1868; and subsequently, deputy of the minister of finance, and secretary of the treasury board; declined the finance ministry, 1862; has been vice-chancellor of the University of Toronto.

LANYON, MAJOR W. OWEN, C.M.G., (1875).— Aide-de-camp to the major-general commanding the forces, Jamaica, Aug. 1865, till March, 1868; aide-de-camp to Sir J. P. Grant, K.C.B., governor of Jamaica, April, 1868; appointed private secretary, August, 1868; volunteered for service on Gold Coast 1873; for some time acted as colonial secretary; afterwards aide-de-camp to Sir G. Wolseley; despatched on special service to the Gold Coast to act as colonial secretary, November, 1874; sat in the executive and legislative councils; promoted to be a regimental major, April, 1874; administrator of the government, Griqualand West 1875.
LARKUM, H. A.— Foreman of government factory, Ceylon, 1857; acting office assistant, Nov. 1868; resumed duties at the factory, Nov. 1871.
LARTIGUE, R.—Appointed additional clerk in treasury St. Lucia, 1873.
LAUDER, CHARLES R.—Clerk, President's office. Dominica, December, 1872; fourth revenue office, November, 1874.

LAURENCE, E. H.— Resident magistrate, Greenough, Western Australia, March, 1870; was clerk of councils, and private secretary to the acting governor, Nov. 1868, to Sept. 1869; clerk in governor's office and various departments from March, 1863.
LAURIE, ARCHIBALD CAMPBELL.—Appointed district judge of Kandy, July, 1873; is a member of the Faculty of Advocates of Scotland; called in 1860.
LAVERS, JAMES.—Appointed clerk in the customs at Mauritius, Oct. 1857; promoted, Feb. 1859; senior clerk, Sept. 1865.

LAWSON, W. T. G., C.E., &c., M.S.A., Assoc. G.N.A., &c., London.—Entered the service of colonial surveyors department, Sierra Leone, January, 1867; appointed assistant colonial surveyor for and one of the commissioners for crown land in British Sherbro, April, 1867; acting colonial surveyor for Sierra Leone, May to August, 1868; assistant colonial surveyor, 1869; appointed surveyor in charge of Cape Coast works, during the Ashanti expedition, 1873 to 1874; was nominated to survey and relay out the town of Elmina, bombarded during the disturbance, consequent upon the transfer in 1873; engaged in works connected with the transfer of the head-quarters from Cape Coast to Accra.
LAYARD, C. H.—Deputy assistant commissary, Galle, Ceylon, Nov. 1863; assistant colonial storekeeper, 1867; acted as superintendent of police, southern province, from July to Dec. 1872.

LAYARD, SIR C. P., K.C.M.G. (1876), (C.M.G., 1871).—Was formerly at St. John's College, Cambridge; appointed extra-assistant in the colonial secretary's office in Ceylon, 1830; fiscal and magistrate at Jaffna, 1831; assistant to the collector of Colombo, 1832; assistant government agent and district judge, at Kalutara, 1836; assistant government agent and district judge, Negombo, 1837; district judge, Galle, 1839; district judge of Trincomalee, 1840; district judge of Galle, 1850; government agent, western province, 1851; held acting appointment of colonial secretary in conjunction with his own office of government agent for the western province from April to 30th June, 1869, and again from 6th April to 5th Sept. 1871.

LAYARD, EDGAR LEOPOLD, C.M.G., (1875).— Whilst Her Majesty's consul in Fiji, was concerned in the negotiations which resulted in the cession of the islands to Great Britain; administered the government from the cession, in 1874, until the arrival of Sir A. Gordon, in 1875.
LEAKE, SIR LUKE SAMUEL, KNT. BACH. (1876).—Speaker of the legislative council, Western Australia.

LEARY, SAMUEL.—New Amsterdam, British Guiana; diploma of the College of Physicians, Dublin, 1860; diploma of the Faculty of Physicians and Surgeons, Glasgow, 1855; diploma in Midwifery, Andersonian University, 1852, and Faculty Hall, Glasgow, 1855; J.P. and coroner, British Guiana; medical officer East Canje District, Berbice; surgeon, New Amsterdam jail and police health officer, Port of New Amsterdam; entered government service, 1st July, 1873; late medical officer, Skeldon District, Coventyne, Co., Berbice, 1867 to 1873; Philadelphia District, West Coast, Demerara, 1873 to 1875; surgeon, ship, "Caroline Coventry," Africa, East Indies and China, 1855 to 1857; Pettijo and Clonelly Dispensary Districts, Co. Donegrel, Ireland, 1857 to 1867.
LECKIE, PETER.—Member of the legislative council, British Honduras, 1873.

LEE, L. F.—Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, June, 1864; commissioner of requests, Panadura, 1866; assistant government agent, Colombo, Feb. 1871; district judge, Kégalla, Dec. 1871; district judge, Badulla, Sept. 1872; acting as police magistrate, Galle.
LEE, PERCY, M.R.C.S.E.—Appointed assistant colonial surgeon, Ceylon, 1868.

LEES, C. C., C.M.G. (1876).—Was private secretary and aide-de-camp to the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, had local rank of lieut.-colonel, and brigade-major to the militia; entered the army in June, 1854; appointed adjutant to the 2nd battalion Royal Welch Fusiliers on its formation in 1858; sold out in Jan. 1866; and was appointed to the adjutancy of the 3rd administrative battalion Derbyshire rifle volunteers; resigned in May, 1867; appointed collector of customs, Lagos, Nov. 1872; administrator of the government of Lagos, July, 1874, and also lieutenant-governor of the Gold Coast Colony, Nov. 1874; administered the general government, 1876.

LEFEVRE, K.C.B. (Civil). (Creat. 1857.) SIR JOHN GEORGE SHAW LEFEVRE.—Educated at Eton and Trinity College, Camb., where he was senior wrangler, 1818; fellow of Trinity, 1819; graduated M.A. 1821; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1824; elected a bencher, 1855; appointed under-secretary of state for the colonies, 1833; poor-law commissioner, 1834; joint-assistant secretary to the board of trade, 1841; deputy-clerk of the parliaments, 1848; clerk of the parliaments, 1858; is an ecclesiastical commissioner, and vice-chancellor of the university of London; was a civil service commissioner, but resigned 1862.

LE FEUVRE, W. S.—Ensign Hampshire militia, 1853; lieutenant Hampshire militia (embodied), 1854; ensign 77th regiment, 1854; lieutenant 77th regiment, 1855; assistant instructor, Hythe School of Musketry, 1855; instructor of musketry to 2nd brigade light division, Crimea, 1855-6; thanked in general orders; adjutant 77th regiment, 1856; resigned 1857; captain 2nd Hants volunteer rifles, 1860; secretary municipal council Kandy, Ceylon, 1866-73; acting superintendent of police, central province, Ceylon, 1871-1873; superintendent of police, head-quarters, Colombo; northern, eastern
Digitized by
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
and north-western provinces, Ceylon; also in charge of police stores, and pay departments, 1873.
LEFROY, A. O'GRADY.—Appointed treasurer of Western Australia, 1856; was private secretary to Governor Fitzgerald, 1849 to 1855; acting colonial secretary, 1875.

LEFROY, MAJOR-GENERAL JOHN HENRY, R.A., C.B.
