Wetherall, K.S.I. and C.B., at the storm and capture of Rampore Kussia; took part in the operations under Lord Clyde. leading to the surrender of Ameathie, evacuation of Shunkanpore and flight of Bein Malhoo; proceeded with the column under Brigadier Taylor, C.B., and Sir Hope Grant to Fyzabad; present at the crossing of the Gozra Afains of Muchleegawn and Kumdatoti, and pursuit of the rebels into Nepaul (mentioned and thanked in despatches, medal and brevet-major); in 1860 proceeded to Ceylon as assistant military secretary to the late Major-Gen. T. O'Brien, commanding the forces, and for two years governor of that colony, remaining there for three and a half years, when he returned to India, and was appointed to the staff of the Bengal army as brigade-major, Gwalior district, from which post he was invalided home in May, 1866, after a continuous service in the East of seventeen and a-half years; appointed in May, 1867, inspector-general of police, Mauritius; mentioned in despatches from the governor to the secretary of state, especially for his exertions during the dreadful hurricane of March, 1868, &c.; appointed poor-law commissioner and director of the Orphan Asylum, 7th February, 1870; in May, 1870, was selected by Sir Henry Barkly to be his extra A.D.C., and in this capacity was attached to H.R.H. the Duke of Edinburgh, during his stay in the Island.

O'BRIEN, JAMES W.—Appointed clerk and sorter in the imperial post-office, Trinidad, in 1842; held that appointment till 1851; in charge of entire department from 1851 to 1858; and was also employed during that period as clerk in the colo-
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

nial post-office; acted as Her Majesty's packet agent in 1858; in Jan. 1860, on the amalgamation of the imperial and colonial post-offices, was appointed chief clerk of the general post-office; promoted to chief clerk in auditor-general's department 17th April, 1862; was acting postmaster-general from July to Sept. 1862; acting auditor-general from 4th October to 31st December, 1865; acting auditor-general, 1867; appointed to act as auditor-general in 1872; was secretary to the central road board, June, 1869, to March, 1875; acting warden of Couva ward union, and supervisor of the county of Carvin for the purpose of investigating the accounts of the late warden, from Oct. to June, 1873; again acted as auditor-general in 1874; acted as clerk of the legislative council, July to October, 1875.

O'CONNOR, JAMES LYNCH.—Appointed justice of the peace for the county of Caroni, Trinidad, 23rd July, 1863; appointed stipendiary magistrate for the county of St. David and the ward of Blanchisseuse, in the county of St. George, and warden of the Toco ward union, 1st July, 1868.
O'CONNOR, LEON D.—Crown solicitor, Trinidad.
O'CONNOR, W. A.—Appointed government medical officer, Jamaica, 1873.
OFFICER, SIR ROBERT, M.A., Kt. (1869).—Entered the service as colonial surgeon, 1826; speaker of the house of assembly of Tasmania.

O'HALLORAN, JOS. SYLVESTER.—Appointed clerk in audit office, South Australia, 1859; clerk of executive council, and clerk to the court of appeals, 1869; acted also as private secretary to the Right Hon. Sir Jas. Fergusson, Bt., Governor of South Australia from 23rd February, to 18th May, 1870.
O'MAHONY, J.—Appointed third tide-waiter, customs' department, Natal, 1860; second landing-waiter in that department, 1863.

O'MALLEY, EDWARD LOUGHLIN.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, graduated; B.A., 1864; M.A., 1868; called to the bar (Middle Temple), 1866; joined the Norfolk circuit; unsuccessfully contested Bedford, 1868; attorney-general of Jamaica, 1876.

OMMANNEY, M. F.—Lieutenant R.E., educated at Cheltenham and R.M.A., Woolwich; entered the royal engineers, Jan., 1864; employed under the War Office and Admiralty, and at the royal military academy, from 1867 to 1874; private secretary to the Earl of Carnarvon, secretary of state for the Colonies, 4th March, 1874, to 1st January, 1877, when he was appointed a crown agent for the colonies.

ONDAATJE, REV. S. D. J.—Appointed colonial chaplain of Jaffna, Ceylon, Dec. 1840; resigned that appointment, September, 1842; appointed colonial chaplain of St. Thomas' Church, Colombo, in that colony, Dec. 1861.

ONDAATJE, W. C.—Lic. Med. and Surg., Bengal College; appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon; 3rd class, 1846; 2nd class, 1851; 1st class, 1856 and assistant colonial surgeon, 1858; assistant colonial surgeon, 1st class, 1867.

ONTARIO, 1st BISHOP of, 1861.—Dr. J. T. LEWIS, LL.D.—The diocese of Ontario comprises that part of West Canada which is bounded on the north by the Ottawa river, on the south by the Lake of Ontario; eastward from and including Prince Edward Island; on the south-east by the St. Lawrence river and the district of Vandreuil. It is a subdivision of the old diocese of Toronto. Its population is 370,000.
ORANGE RIVER, 2nd Bishop. RIGHT REV. D. MERRIMAN.

ORD, SIR HARRY ST. GEORGE, C.B. (1865), KNT., (1867).—Entered the royal engineers, 1837; promoted to be lieut., 1839; captain, 1846; brevet-major, 1854; lieut.-col., 1859; major-gen., 1869; served principally in the West Indies and coast of Africa; served as brigade-major in the combined French and English expedition to the Baltic in 1854; and was at the siege and capture of Bomarsund, for which he received brevet rank; was employed under the colonial office in 1855-6 as commissioner to the Gold Coast; and in 1856-57 and 1860 at the courts of Paris and the Hague, to assist the ambassador and minister in carrying out negotiations with respect to the French and Netherlands possessions on the coast of Africa; appointed lieut.-governor of Dominica, Aug. 1857; and governor and commander-in-chief of Bermuda, Feb. 1861; sent on a special mission to the settlements on the West Coast of Africa, Oct. 1864; governor of Straits Settlements. 1867 to Nov. 1873.

ORD, A. R.—Entered the 54th regiment, Dec., 1851; promoted lieutenant 86th regiment, 1855, and to a company, without purchase, in the 65th regiment, 1862; served during the Indian mutiny of 1857-8-9; was present at the action of Rota-ki-Lerai, at the battle and siege and capture of the town and fortress of Gwalior; commanded two companies 86th regiment at the siege and capture of the fort of Powrie; medal and clasp for Central India; served in the New Zealand war of 1863; medal for New Zealand, also medal for Bhootan; was present and commanded a detachment 65th regiment at the attack near the Williamson's clearing; commanded the Fort Alexander, and repulsed two attacks on that post; retired from the army in 1867; and appointed police magistrate, Malacca, Straits Settlements, 10th Jan., 1867; administered the lieut.-government Malacca, from 3rd July to 12th September, 1868; from 19th Dec. 1868 to 3rd February, 1869; from 25th May, 1872 to 22nd March, 1873; from 29th March to 19th April, 1874.

O'REILLY, PETER.—Formerly a lieutenant in the revenue police in Ireland; appointed assistant gold commissioner and stipendiary magistrate of British Columbia, April, 1859; high sheriff, 1859; legislative councillor, 1863; and chief commissioner, Jan. 1864; magistrate of Yale, and visiting commissioner of the Kootenay gold district, May, 1867.

ORGIAS, PAULIN, M.D., M.R.C.S., London.—Medical officer of St. Andrew's parish, Grenada, 1858; surgeon of colony hospital and medical officer of the town of St. George, 1867; health officer and surgeon of royal gaol, 1870; has been a member of the legislative assemblies since 1868; is a J.P.
ORMSBY, R. D.—Superintending office, public works department, Ceylon, 1866; acting provincial assistant north-western province from July, 1870, to May, 1871.

ORPEN, A. R.—Civil commissioner, resident magistrate and sub-collector of customs, East London, appointed July 1, 1870; was resident magistrate and sub-collector of customs, Hondeklip Bay, 1862 to 1870; has served in the customs department since 1858.

ORPEN, FRANCIS HENRY SAMUEL, F.R.G.S.—Surveyor-general of the province of Griqualand West, 1872; one of the commission appointed June, 1850, for surveying the Orange River sovereignty; entered the service of the Griqua Chief, N. Waterboer, March, 1867, as civil commissioner, resident
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

magistrate, and surveyor-general of the Settlement of Albania, and, on the cession of Griqualand West to the British Government, was appointed civil commissioner of the division of Hay (then Griquatown) Nov. 1871.

ORPEN, HENRY MARTYN HERBERT.—Served as captain commanding Colesbey native levies, from January, 1851, to August, 1853, during the Kaffir war of those years; also as district adjutant of North Victoria from April, 1852, to March, 1853; held several other military appointments during the same period; received a Kaffir war medal for services in the field; appointed to the customs' department at Port Elizabeth, Cape Colony, Dec. 1853; measuring surveyor under merchant shipping Act, Jan. 1856; landing surveyor, August, 1857; sub-collector and surveyor and comptroller of H.M. customs and navigation laws, Cape Town, October, 1867; appointed to temporary charge of customs' department, April, 1868, upon retirement of collector and principal comptroller until appointment of his successor.

ORTEZ, MANUEL M.D.—Educated at the University of Caraccas, in Venezuela; surgeon of the Civil Hospital, Bolivar, 1854; principal medical director of the National College of Guiana, 1855; health officer and physician of the Military Hospital of Bolivar, 1860; dresser in Dreadnought Hospital, Greenwich, England, 1863; resident surgeon Birmingham Lying-in Hospital, 1864; L.F.P.S.G. and L.M. Glasgow, 1865; honorary member of the Society of Arts and Industry, London, 1866; fellow of the Obstetrical Society, London, 1866; member of the Medical Congress, Madrid, 1867; appointed acting house surgeon, Colonial Hospital, Trinidad, 25th Feb. 1867.
OSBORN, M.—Clerk to resident magistrate at Ladismith, Natal; appointed Sept. 1865, resident magistrate of Newcastle in that colony.

O'SHANASSY, SIR JOHN K.C.M.G. (1874), (C.M.G. 1869).—Emigrated from Ireland to Australia soon after the first settlement of Port Phillip; he took a part in the municipal government of the city of Melbourne, and was elected a member of the first legislative council of the district; became chief secretary upon the 11th March, 1857, but retired from office at the end of the following month; held the same position from March, 1858, until Oct. 1859, and again from Nov. 1861, to June, 1863. Upon the eve of his departure for England, in 1866, a service of plate, worth more than £1,500, was presented to him by general subscription, in recognition of his public services.
OTWAY, LOFTUS B.—Postmaster, Grenada, December, 1874; member of the legislative assembly since 1866.
OUDKERK, G., JUN.—Fifth clerk in the office of the colonial book-keeper, British Guiana, 1866.

OUDTSHOORN, G. Van R. Van.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Stellenbosch division of the Cape of Good Hope, appointed Dec. 1867; was clerk to the magistrate at Wynberg from 1841 to 1849; clerk to civil commissioner of Swellendam, Feb. 1849 to 1859; civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Swellendam, 1859 to 1867.

OWEN, A. DOUGLAS.—Acting clerk, immigration office, Trinidad, 14th May, 1872; temporary clerk, colonial secretary's office, 11th June, 1872; fourth clerk, colonial secretary's office, 1st January, 1873; acting inspector of police, 10th June, 1873; acting private secretary to Governor Longden, C.M.G., from 1st September, 1873, to February, 1874; appointed inspector of police, 24th December, 1873.
PACK, H.—Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, Nov. 1860.

PACKER, CHARLES.—Graduated B.A. Trinity College, Cambridge, 1839; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1841; appointed solicitor-general, Barbados, Jan. 1847; escheator-general, 1859; elected member of the general assembly for the parish of St. Andrew's, 1846; and has continued to represent the same constituency to the present time; elected speaker of house of assembly, 1861; member of legislative council, 1868; chief justice of Barbados, 1874.

PADMORE, W.—Clerk in customs, Jamaica, 1839; clerk and warehouse keeper, St. Christopher, 1842; acted as collector of customs, 1845 and 1848; clerk and assistant comptroller of customs and navigation laws, Barbados, 1853; acted as comptroller, 1854; pensioned, Jan. 1855, on abolition of customs in West Indies; notary public, St. Christopher, 1858; superintendent of immigrants, 1858; private secretary to Sir B. C. C. Pine; clerk to executive council and administrative committee, 17th June, 1865; clerk to office of auditor-general, 1866; provost-marshal, 1868. Resigned office of superintendent of immigrants, 1870.
PAKENHAM, T. C.—Was appointed district clerk at Port Louis, Mauritius, Feb. 1857; clerk to the chief judge, June, 1857; and appointed curator of vacant estates, Jan. 1859; consul at Madagascar.
PALMER, EDWARD.—Chief Justice of Prince Edward Island; has been attorney- and solicitor-general, and has sat in the local parliament.
PALMER, T. C., M.R.C.S.—Medical officer of district St. Kitts.

PARGITER, R. S.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1867; commissioner of requests, &c., Point Pedro, 1868; acting commissioner of requests, &c., Chavakachcheri, 1868; police magistrate, Kalpitiya, 1869, to continue to act at Chavakachcheri; acting assistant government agent, Jaffna, 1870; assistant government agent, Galle, Dec. 1871, continuing to act at Jaffna; acting assistant government agent Nuwera Eliya, May, 1872; acting landing surveyor, Colombo, June, 1872.

PARKER, C. S.—Educated at Eton and University College, Oxford; first-class in classics, M.A., fellow of University College; public examiner, 1859, 1860, and 1863; examiner in modern languages, 1862-3; curator of the university galleries; appointed private secretary to Mr. Cardwell, May, 1864, to July, 1866; M.P. for Perthshire, 1868; a special commissioner for public schools, 1868; and a commissioner for military education, 1869.

PARKER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858.) SIR HENRY WATSON PARKER.—Was private secretary to Sir George Gipps when governor of New South Wales; was for many years a member and chairman of committee of the legislative council; was first minister and principal secretary and a member of the executive council of New South Wales. Unsuccessfully contested Greenwich, Dec. 1868.

PARKER, WILLIAM ALEXANDER.—Of the Scottish bar; is a member of the general council of the University of Edinburgh; in 1839 he was appointed assistant extractor of judicial records in her Majesty's general register house, Edinburgh; was honorary secretary of the architectural institute of Scotland; and corresponding associate for Scotland of the society of antiquaries of London, and of the genealogical and historical society of Great Britain; in 1853 was admitted a member of the faculty of advocates of Scotland; in 1859 was appointed captain of No. 1 battery, Midlothian
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

coast artillery volunteers; in 1866 was appointed chief magistrate and member of the legislative council of her Majesty's forts and settlements on the Gold Coast, and judicial assessor to the native chiefs of the Gold Coast protectorate, Western Africa; in 1869 was appointed chief justice and judge of the Vice-admiralty court of St. Helena; member of executive council, 1871; chief justice, British Honduras, 1875.

PARSONS, J.—Held office of deputy fiscal at Kandy, in Ceylon, 1846; commissioner of requests, &c., Matara, 1851; assistant to the government agent, Kandy, 1851; commissioner of requests, Puttalam, 1854; acting district judge, Tangalle, 1855; assistant government agent, &c., &c. Hambantota, 1856; acting principal collector of customs, 1866; acting government agent, southern province, 1868; government agent, north-western province, and acting principal collector of customs, 1869; assumed duties as government agent for the north-western provinces March, 1870; acting government agent, southern province, April, 1872; government agent, central province, July, 1872.
PATERSON, Dr. A. S.—Colonial surgeon, South Australia.

PATERSON, G. W.—Educated at the University of Glasgow, where he graduated M.A. 1857, being first in the order of merit in mathematics and natural philosophy; appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Feb. 1859, having obtained the writership presented to the Glasgow University by the lord rector, Sir Edward Bulwer Lytton, then secretary of state for the colonies; acting police magistrate, Point Pedro, March, 1862, in which appointment he was confirmed, May, 1862; assistant government agent, Colombo, Jan. 1865; district judge, Tangalla, June, 1867; assistant government agent, Puttalam, June, 1873.

PATEY, VICE-ADMIRAL CHARLES GEORGE EDWARD, C.M.G. (1874).—Entered the navy, 1824; lieut. 1836; served in the operations on the coast of Syria; commander, 1840; captain, 1846; appointed emigration officer at Glasgow, 1850; removed to Liverpool on the occasion of the great Australian emigration, 1851; received severe injuries whilst in command of H.M.S. 'Amphion,' 1853; appointed emigration officer at Londonderry, 1854; Glasgow, Oct. 1854; Plymouth, 1855 to 1857; was captain superintendent of the packet service, Southampton, from 1857 to 1864; rear-admiral, 1864; appointed administrator of the government of Lagos, 1866; administrator of the Gambia, Oct. 1866; governor, St. Helena, 1869 to 1873; vice-admiral, 1871; retired on pension, 1873.
PATMAN, F. SHABAEL.—Appointed colonial surveyor, superintendent of public works, &c., &c., Sierra Leone, 14th July, 1868.
PATTERSON, P.—Civil engineer, Natal, 1860; was previously in the service of the colony of Grenada. R. M. Inanda Division, 22nd Nov., 1872.
PATTRICK, JOHN BROOKE, B.A.—Of Wadham College, Oxford, appointed a civil chaplain in Mauritius, in March, 1869.
PAUL, W. F. B.—Educated at Eton; accompanied the late Sir James Brooke, Rajah of Sarawak, to Borneo in 1860. Appointed civil commandant at Accra, West Coast of Africa, in 1873.

PAUNCEFOTE, SIR JULIAN, Knight Bach. (Created 1874).—Called to the bar, Inner Temple, May, 1852, and joined the Oxford circuit; appointed attorney-general of Hong Kong, May, 1865; was ex officio member of the executive and legislative councils of the colony; appointed acting chief justice of the supreme court in 1869 and again in 1872; prepared "The Hong Kong Code of Civil Procedure,"

and other important ordinances now in force in the colony relating to law reform and the constitution of the courts, and to emigration, extradition, and other subjects; received the thanks of the legislative council of Hong Kong and the honour of knighthood for his public services to the colony; appointed chief justice of the Leeward Islands in 1873, and an assistant under-secretary of state for the colonies, 28 September, 1874, to 29th June, 1876; an assistant under-secretary of state, foreign office, 30th June, 1876.

PAYNE, JOHN AUGUSTUS.—Appointed commissioner of the petty debt court of Lagos, 28th August, 1863; clerk of police court, Jan., 1866; registrar of births, marriages, and deaths, 1st July, 1867; promoted to be clerk of court, civil and criminal justice, and performing the duties of sheriff, 13th Nov., 1869; clerk of court of requests, 1st Jan., 1871; appointed clerk of courts on the amalgamation of the two offices of the police court and clerk of the superior court, 7th Nov., 1872; author of "Payne's Lagos Almanack."
PEACHEY, JOHN JAMES.—Acting third clerk treasury, Singapore, October, 1872; chief clerk, treasury, Malacca, November, 1872.
PEARCE, G. H.—Employed as clerk in penitentiary, Jamaica, 1860 to 1864; clerk in private secretary's office, 1864-5, when he was appointed to post-office; chief clerk, post-office, 1875.

PEARSE, BENJAMIN W.—Assistant surveyor-general, Vancouver Island, appointed 1861; previously employed in same capacity by Hudson Bay Company during their tenure of the island, 1868; now resident engineer for Dominion Public Works, in Victoria.

PEARSON, ARTHUR A.—Appointed 1867, after a competitive examination, to be a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies; second class clerk, 30th Sept. 1872; appointed assistant private secretary to the Earl of Carnarvon, 9 March, 1874.
PEARSON, CHARLES W.—Sub-collector of customs at Port Alfred, Cape of Good Hope, April, 1869.

PEEL, Privy Councillor (1857), K.C.M.G. (1869).—RIGHT HONOURABLE SIR FREDERICK PEEL, 2nd son of the late Sir Robert Peel, 2nd Bart.—Educated at Harrow and at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was 1st class in classics, 1845; was called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1849; was under-secretary for the colonies from Nov. 1851 to March, 1852, and from Dec. 1852 to Feb. 1855; under-secretary for war from 1855 to 1857; is a deputy-lieut. of Warwickshire; was M.P. for Leominster from Feb. 1849 to July, 1852, and for Bury, Lancashire, from July, 1852 to April, 1857; re-elected for Bury, May, 1859, and appointed secretary to the treasury, 1860, railway commissioner, 1873.

PEEL, JOHN BARDSLEY.—Entered the survey department in February, 1861; appointed assistant-surveyor of Singapore, April, 1863; acting assistant-surveyor of Malacca, January, 1868; assistant-surveyor of Penang, November, 1868.
PEMBERTON, AUGUSTUS.—Commissioner of police and stipendiary magistrate at Victoria, Vancouver's Island, appointed 1858.

PEMBERTON, MORDAUNT.—Called to the bar of Nevis, 1864; appointed Queen's counsel, 1869; called to the bar of St. Christopher, 1869; acting solicitor-general of Nevis, July, 1870; member of the legislative assembly and executive council of the same island.
PEMBERTON, SHOLTO THOS.—Chief justice of Dominica, appointed 1861; was previously soli-
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
citor-general of the island of Nevis, 1850; second puisne judge, Leeward Islands, 1873; chief justice, Bahamas, 1876.

PENNELL, E. B.—Appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1859; promoted to the 3rd class, April, 1863; private secretary to Mr. Forster, under-secretary of state for the colonies, until July 6th, 1866; also to Sir C. Adderley, from that date to October 1, and to Lord Blachford from 1 Oct. 1866; promoted to be an assistant clerk, 20th May, 1867; 1st class clerk, 30th Sept. 1872; proceeded to Paris on special service, 1874.

PENNY, W.—Educated at Trinity College, Glenalmond; appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, April, 1871; acting police magistrate, Gampola, Aug. 1873; police magistrate, Panadura, Sept. 1873, to continue to act at Gampola; acting police magistrate, Matali, Oct. 1873.

PENNYCUICK, C. E. D.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, Nov. 1866; acting landing surveyor, Galle, March, 1867; police magistrate, &c., Panvilla, Feb. 1868; assistant government agent, Batticaloa, March, 1871; acting district judge, &c., Badulla, May, 1871.
PERKIS, A. E.—Consular clerk, Hong Kong; appointed 1862. Transferred to Pekin, under foreign office.
PERTH (WESTERN AUSTRALIA), 2nd BISHOP of. Founded 1856.—RIGHT REV. HENRY HUTTON PARRY, D.D.—Appointed, 1876; consecrated, 1868.

PHAYRE, MAJOR-GENERAL SIR ARTHUR PURVES, K.C.S.I. (1867), C.B. (1863).—Served in the 7th Bengal native infantry, and became lieut.-col. Bengal staff corps; made major-general in the army in 1871; was British commissioner for Arracan, and afterwards for many years chief commissioner at British Burmah; was created a knight of the Star of India for his long and efficient services in Burmah, where he concluded several important treaties; retired, and returned to England, 1867; appointed governor of Mauritius, Oct. 1874.
PHILIP, M. M.—Solicitor-general, Trinidad, March, 1871; acted as attorney-general, June, 1873, to July, 1874.

PHILLIPPO, GEORGE.—Admitted to the honourable society of the Inner Temple, April, 1859; obtained a certificate of honour of the first class, Jan., 1862; called to the bar, Hilary term, 1862; admitted to the Jamaica bar, June, 1862; practised at the Jamaica bar until Feb., 1868; during that time was professionally engaged before the royal and special commissioners appointed in consequence of the serious disturbances in that island, and of the measures of repression taken by the Government; was detained in England for some months by the Jamaica committee in order to give evidence as to the laws of Jamaica in the various prosecutions instituted by that body; appointed queen's advocate, Sierra Leone, March, 1868, and acting judge of the court of summary jurisdiction during the absence on leave of Mr. Justice Huggins, August, 1868; appointed attorney-general of British Columbia, March, 1870; appointed on a commission to collect, revise, and publish a complete edition of the laws of that colony, 1871; prepared the proclamation necessary for bringing the new constitution of that colony into operation under the order in council, 1870, whereby representative institutions were to some extent conceded; nominated a non-elective member of the legislative council under the new constitution, Dec., 1870; prepared the address from the legislature of British Columbia to Her Majesty for union with Canada, and pre-

pared and passed through the legislative council, under the direction of the government, "The Constitution Act, 1871," and the several other measures necessary to enable British Columbia to enter into confederation with Canada, with full representative institutions and under "responsible government;" puisne judge of the supreme court of British Guiana, May, 1871; went to Gibraltar on special service, Sept., 1873; appointed acting attorney-general and judge of the court of requests, Gibraltar, during the absence on leave of F. Solly Flood, Esq.; remained in Gibraltar after the return of Mr. Flood, on special service from Dec., 1873, to Feb., 1874; appointed junior puisne judge, Straits Settlements, Dec., 1873; and senior puisne judge, Straits Settlements, March, 1874; attorney-general, Hong Kong, August, 1876.

PHILLIPS, C. H.—Acting chief clerk, registrar-general's department, Trinidad, Oct., 1870, and April, 1871, and May, 1872; acting superintendent registrar, births and deaths, Oct., 1870, and April, 1871; and May, 1872; was for many years chief clerk in the marshal's department; chief clerk in registrar-general's department, and superintendent registrar of births and deaths, July, 1873.

PHILPOTT, EDWARD.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Uitenhage division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1867. Was clerk to the magistrate at Port Elizabeth, 1837 to 1842; resigned on account of ill-health; re-appointed clerk to civil commissioner of Port Elizabeth, March, 1848; appointed civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Alexandria, August, 1857; ditto Cradock, 1866 to 1867.
PICKEL, W. A.—Appointed 2nd clerk in police magistrate's office, British Guiana, 1873.

PICKWOAD, ROBERT WILLIAMS.—Appointed private secretary to W. W. Cairns, Esq., when lieut.-governor of British Honduras, 28th July, 1870; continued in that capacity from June, 1871, to June, 1872, to Colonel Harley, who administered the government during Mr. Cairns' leave of absence; appointed clerk to the colonial secretary and clerk to the legislative council of British Honduras, 28th March, 1872; continued as private secretary to Mr. Cairns on His Excellency's return from leave in June, 1872, to March, 1874; private secretary to Captain Mitchell, R.M., during his administration of the government, to Major Mundy on his arrival in May, 1874, to September of the same year, and again subsequently to Captain Mitchell. Appointed keeper of cemeteries, 16th November, 1874.

PIERRE, LOUIS PHILIP.—Appointed clerk of the peace of the county of Caroni, Trinidad, November, 1860; clerk of the peace of the county of Victoria, with the town of San Fernando, in 1869; clerk of the peace of Port of Spain and the western district of the county of St. George in June, 1869; appointed acting stipendiary magistrate of the county of St. David, and ward of Blanchisseuse in the county of St. George, and warden of the Toco Ward Union, in June, 1872; and on the 14th of January, 1874, stipendiary magistrate of the county of St. David and ward of Blanchisseuse, warden of the Toco Ward Union, and acting stipendiary magistrate of Arouca and Arima in the eastern district of the county of St. George.

PIGUENIT, JAMES GEORGE.—Acting assistant master at grammar school, St. Christopher, 1865; clerk to secretary to government, 1866; clerk to board of health, 1867; clerk to the president as pre-auditor, 1871; notary public, 1873.
PIKE, CHARLES.—Appointed acting second clerk in the treasury, Sierra Leone, August, 1869; chief clerk of customs, Lagos, 1870; acting second clerk
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
and cashier, treasury, 1871; acting deputy collector, part of 1872 to 1875; acting assistant collector and treasurer, 1875.

PINE, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856), K.C.M.G. (1871).—SIR BENJAMIN CHILLEY CAMPBELL PINE. —Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1834, M.A. 1840; called to the bar, Gray's Inn, 1841; appointed Queen's advocate at Sierra Leone, 1841; acting governor there, 1848, accompanied Capt. Monypenny, R.N., to the Sherbro river, and destroyed a strong stockaded fort, from which outrages had been committed on British trading vessels. In 1849, again proceeded to the Sherbro, in conjunction with Capt. Dunlop, R.N., and a detachment of troops, and put an end to the civil war, which had ravaged the country for years; lieut.-governor of Natal, 1849; 1851 received the thanks of her majesty's government for the measures he took to preserve peace in the colony during the Kaffir war; in 1852 despatched a force, consisting of Cape mounted rifles, two companies of the 45th regiment, and 900 Zulus, to the aid of British troops in the Orange River sovereignty, who were reported to be acting on the defensive against an overpowering force of hostile tribes; superintended in person the raising of the Zulu force, and accompanied the expedition over the Drackenbent mountains in mid winter; received the thanks of Sir Henry Smith for these services; in 1855, led a force of mounted volunteers against the Amabaca tribe, numbering 2,000 men, and completely enforced their submission; governor and commander-in-chief of the Gold Coast settlements, 1856; lieut.-governor of St. Christopher's, May, 1859. Is author of some articles in the Encyclopedia Britannica on the 'African Colonies;' acting governor, Antigua, 1866; governor in-chief of the Leeward Islands, Feb. 1869. Created K.C.M.G. for his services in connection with the confederation of the Leeward Islands; lieut.-governor of Natal, 1873, until 1875; when he retired on a governor's pension.
PLAGEMANN, F. O.—Clerk in treasury department, St. Kitt's, 1866-9; third landing waiter, 1873; now 2nd landing waiter.
PLAGEMANN, O. F.—Superintendent of police, St. Christopher, March, 1859; superintendent of public works, June, 1859; town warden, Basse-terre, 1872.
PLAICE, G.—Draftsman and framer of estimates, public works department, Ceylon, 1866.

PLUES, SAMUEL SEVIRE.—Was chief clerk in the governor's secretary's office, Jamaica, 25th July, 1843, to 31st Aug., 1854; was appointed police magistrate of British Honduras, 11th Sept., 1854; clerk of courts and keeper of public records, 19th Aug., 1862; attorney-general for the colony, 12th Nov., 1864; and queen's advocate-general or advocate in the court of vice-admiralty, 27th April, 1865.
PLUMMER, H. P.—Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana.
PLUNKETT, THE HON. C. B.—Appointed commissioner of the court of requests, Penang, 29th Oct. 1862; commissioner of police, Straits Settlements, 1871; registrar of supreme court, Hong Kong, 1876.

POLE, REGINALD C.—Was educated at Marlborough college; employed from July, 1859, till Sept. 1861, in the surveyor-general's department, during the latter part on the trigonometrical survey, Ceylon; acting landing and tide surveyor, at Galle, April, 1862; acting assistant collector of customs at Jaffna; appointed landing surveyor, Galle, 1st April, 1863; acting assistant govern-

ment agent, Jaffna; June, 1863; confirmed, 1865; passed final examination in languages (Tamil), laws and accounts, Jan. 1868; police magistrate and commissioner of requests, Galle, Feb. 1868; assistant government agent, Matalé, Nov. 1868; police magistrate of Kandy, Sept. 1869; acting assistant government agent of the north-western province at Puttalam, 1st Dec. 1871.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH.—Appointed auditor-general, British Guiana, 1860; was financial accountant from 1853 to 1860, when the office was abolished.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH, jun.—Colonial civil engineer, British Guiana.
POLLARD, W. F. B.—Appointed surgeon to penal settlement, British Guiana, 1873.

POPE, RICHARD.—Born 1827; called to the bar of Lower Canada. 1854; assistant editor of the "Lower Canada Law Reports," 1855 to 1861; president of the Chaudière gold mining association, 1866 to 1871; clerk in corresponding branch, department public works, and private secretary to minister, 1872 until 21st October, 1873, when appointed clerk of the crown in chancery for Canada; is a major in the militia; organized Quebec volunteer rifle association at the time of the "Trent" affair; won first prize medal of Literary and Historical Society, Quebec, for best "Essay on Canada," 1853; author of "Canadian Minerals and Mining Interests," 1857; "Gold Fields of Canada," in 1858; "Notes on Immigration" and "Mining and Agricultural Labour in Canada," 1859.

PORTER, NEALE.—Presiding magistrate of Anguilla from March 1869 to May 1871; acting colonial secretary from May 1871, to Oct. 1871; acting lieut.-governor of Dominica from Oct. 3, 1871, to March 31, 1872; acting president of Dominica from April 1 to June 13, 1872; president, Montserrat, June 13, 1872.
PORTER, W., C.M.G. (1872).—Formerly attorney-general of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed Sept. 1839; retired on a pension, 1865.
PORTER, W. HENRY.—Appointed clerk, president's office, Dominica, November, 1874.

POTTS, MOSES A.—Educated at the Sierra Leone Grammar School. Entered the service in H.M. commissariat at Sierra Leone in June, 1853, and served uninterruptedly in that department till January, 1860; served also in the barrack department at that station; was acting second clerk in the colonial treasury of that settlement from July 1865, to January, 1866; appointed extra clerk in the same department in January, 1866; acting house and land-tax clerk from April 1866, to June 1868; appointed house and land-tax clerk, Aug. 1869; promoted to be second clerk in the colonial treasury, after a competitive examination, on the 15th Sept. 1870; census master for the year 1871; was acting first clerk and cashier from January to May, 1873, and again from August of the same year.

POUND, ALFRED JOHN, M.A., (Oxon.) and F.R.G.S.—Educated at Eton (King's Scholar), and at Exeter College, Oxford, (Exhibitioner); called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 17th November, 1871; practised as equity, draughtsman and conveyancer, and also on the western circuit, and at the Winchester, Hants, Southampton and Portsmouth sessions, till May, 1875; when he was appointed a stipendiary magistrate in British Guiana.
POWNALL, WM. R.—Appointed a clerk in emigration commissioners' office, London, December 1848; promoted to be a senior clerk, 1873.
PRATT, LIEUT.-COLONEL FRANCIS EDWARD,
Digitized by
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
R.E.—Surveyor-general and director of works, Fiji, 1875; member of the executive council, 1876.

PRESGRAVE, D. C.—Acting police magistrate, Penang; chief clerk in the treasury, Penang, 1st July, 1849; appointed to the municipal department as secretary, April, 1851, acting magistrate of police and commissioner of the court of requests, Penang, 24th Oct., 1864; appointed deputy coroner, Feb., 1865; twice appointed acting magistrate and commissioner of the court of requests, Province Wellesley, in 1865; and again appointed acting police magistrate and commissioner, court of requests, Penang, 9th May, 1870; and duly admitted as an advocate in the supreme court of the Straits Settlements.
PRESTAGE, WARD.—Overseer of works, Hong Kong, appointed July, 1863; now clerk of the works in that island.

PRICE, EDWARD WILLIAM.—Entered Royal Navy as naval cadet, 1846; served in Black Sea fleet till close of Russian war, for which he received English and Turkish medals; appointed to civil service, South Australia, 1860; stipendiary magistrate, 1873; government resident, Northern Territory of South Australia, 1876.
PRICE, JOHN M., F.G.S., F.R.G.S.—Appointed March, 1871, chief engineer Sierra Leone harbour works; January, 1873, surveyor-general of Hong Kong.
PRIME, C.—Drainchtsman and framer of estimates, public works department, Ceylon, March, 1866.
PULLICINO, FILIPPO, LL.D.—Appointed one of H.M.'s judges, Malta, 1873.

QUEBEC, 4th Bishop of.—Dr. J. W. WILLIAMS, 1863.—Educated at Crewkerne school, and Pembroke College, Oxford; graduate B.A., 1851, 3rd class classics; curate of High Wycombe, Bucks, 1851; proceeded to Canada to open grammar school in connection with Bishop College, Lennoxville; is a professor in that college; consecrated 4th Bishop of Quebec, 1863; the diocese was first constituted by patent, 28th June, 1793; and comprised Upper and Lower Canada and their dependencies. It now includes districts of Quebec, Three Rivers, St. Francis and Gaspé, and contains 43 parishes.

RAE, JOHN, M.A.—Educated at University of Aberdeen, where he passed as master of arts, 1832, law student, from 1831 to 1839; arrived in New South Wales, 1842, was appointed town clerk of Sydney, 1843; a commissioner for the City of Sydney, 1854; secretary to the railway department, New South Wales, 1857; under secretary for public works and commissioner for railways, 1861.

RAMPINI, CHARLES.—Civil law prizeman, University of Edinburgh, 1865; member of the faculty of advocates, Scotland, June, 1865; stipendiary magistrate for the parishes of Portland, St. Thomas-ye-East and St. George's, Jamaica, March, 1867; judge of the Port Antonio district court, Nov. 1867; judge of the Mandeville district court, May, 1868; is coroner for the parishes of Manchester and Clarendon; and a justice of the peace for those of St. Thomas and Portland.
RANGEL, Q. A.—Clerk in the treasury, Hong Kong, May, 1871.

RANDELL, HENRY LLOYD.—Surgeon major, served with the 62nd regiment in the Crimea from the 4th July, 1855, including the siege and fall of Sebastopol, and assault of the Redan on the 8th September (medal with clasp, and Turkish medal); subsequently served in the West Indies, and went out to Canada with the expeditionary force in January, 1862; and afterwards served in India, till March, 1867; in Nov., 1867, was placed on
the seconded list, on obtaining the appointment of principal civil medical officer, Straits Settlements.

RAWSON, SIR RAWSON WILLIAM, K.C.M.G., (1875) C.B. (1858).—Appointed a clerk in the board of trade, Jan. 1829; served as private secretary to the vice-president, Mr. C. Poulett Thomson, 1830-34; to the president, Mr. Alex. Baring, 1834; and to the vice-president, Mr. W. E. Gladstone, 1841-42; acted as secretary to the pilotage commission, 1834-36; appointed civil secretary to the governor-general of Canada, Aug. 1842; transferred, upon the abolition of that office, to the treasurership of Mauritius, Jan. 1844; appointed colonial secretary of the Cape of Good Hope, May, 1854; appointed governor of the Bahamas, Nov. 1864; received, Nov., 1865, a dormant commission as lieutenant-governor of Jamaica, to act in the absence or incapacity of the captain-general and governor-in-chief; governor-in-chief of Windward Islands, from 1868 till 1875. Retired on pension.

READ, THE VEN. ARCH. J. H., D.D.—1st ARCHDEACON OF PRINCE EDWARD ISLAND, 1860.—Ecclesiastical commissary, 1858; formerly of St. John's College, Cambridge, and B.D., 1852; D.D., 1857, of King's College, Frederickton, New Brunswick.

REES, GEORGE HERMAN.—Entered the Jamaica civil service in the office of the inspector of prisons. 20th April, 1865; principal clerk in the office of the governor's private secretary, 22nd December, 1865; 2nd-class clerk in the colonial secretariat, 8th September, 1866; and principal clerk in the office of the island medical establishment, 1st December, 1871.
REHE, S.—Superintending officer, public works department, Ceylon, 1862.
REID, FRANCIS, M.D.—Deputy inspector-general of hospital (h.p.); chief medical officer, Mauritius, Jan. 1867.

REID, R.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1866; acting police magistrate, Galagedern, 1867; justice of the peace and deputy coroner, Colombo; commissioner of requests, &c., Kalpitya, 1868; assistant government agent, Jaffna, June, 1869; second assistant colonial secretary; continuing to act as district judge, Ratnapura, April, 1872.
REID, W. W.—Non-elective member, for St. Kitts, of general council, Leeward Islands, 1873.

REINECKER, BERNHARD HENRY, B.A. of Gonville and Caius College, Cambridge.—Emigrated to Canterbury, New Zealand, for several years; justice of the peace of that colony; appointed, February, 1875, auditor for the Gold Coast Colony.
RENDELL, STEPHEN.—Appointed a member of the legislative council, Newfoundland, May, 1874.
RENOUF, CHARLES.—Clerk in colonial secretary's office, Mauritius, 1837; clerk to the district magistracy at Flacq, 1858; stipendiary magistrate at Pamplemousses, April, 1862.
REYNOLDS, T. J.—Employed on the unfixed establishment of the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, from 1st May, 1856, till 28th February, 1866; appointed assistant-surveyor on 1st March. 1866.

RICHARDS, ALBERT MORTON, Q.C.—Born, 1822; called to the bar of Upper Canada, 1848; to that of British Columbia, 1871; created a Q.C., 1863; sat in legislative assembly, Canada, from 1863 to 1864, and in house of commons from 1872 until January, 1874; was a member of the executive council, Canada, and solicitor-general for Upper Canada, 1863-4; appointed attorney-general for Rupert's Land, and the north-west territories, October, 1869; legal adviser in British Columbia for the dominion government, 1874;
Digitized by Google
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
lieutenant-governor of the province of British Columbia, 20th July, 1876.

RICHARDS, STEPHEN.—Born, 1820; called to the bar of Upper Canada, 1844; created a Q.C., 1858; elected a bencher of law society of Ontario, 1871; sat in Ontario assembly from 1867 to 1875, and was commissioner of crown lands from July, 1867, to July, 1875; and provincial secretary and registrar from latter date to Dec., 1871.

RICHARDS, HON. WILLIAM BUELL.—Born 2nd May, 1815; educated at the Johnstown district grammar school; called to the bar of Upper Canada in Michaelmas Term, 1837; elected a bencher of the Law Society, 1849; created a Q.C., 1850; was a member of executive council, Canada, and attorney-general for Upper Canada, from 28th October, 1851, to 21st June, 1853, when appointed a puisne judge of the court of common pleas, where he remained until 22nd July, 1873, when promoted to be chief justice of that court; appointed chief justice of Ontario, 16th November, 1868; chief justice of the supreme court of the dominion, 8th October, 1875; and deputy-governor of the dominion, 29th July, 1876; was arbitrator on behalf of Ontario, for the settlement of the north-western boundary of the province, 1874.
RICHARDSON, SIR JOHN LARKINS CHEESE, KNIGHT BACH. (1875).—Speaker of the legislative council of New Zealand; lieut.-colonel of the local militia, and late brevet major of Her Majesty's Indian forces.
RICHARDSON, OLIVER K.—Appointed second clerk, colonial secretary's office, South Australia, 1841; chief clerk, 1842; assistant colonial secretary, 1853; under secretary, 1856.

RICKWOOD, A. G.—Employed in the surveyor general's department, Mauritius, from 1846 to 1850; quitted the public service, and accepted private employment, from 1851, to 1855, when he rejoined the public service; tax clerk, internal revenue, November, 1856; second clerk, despatch branch, colonial secretary's office, February, 1857; superintendent of distilleries, September, 1862.
RILEY, SERJEANT.—Appointed superintendent of police, Dominica, 1873.

RIPOLL, CHARLES LEWIS.—Sub-agent of immigration of the second class, Jamaica, Oct., 1872; entered the colonial service as clerk in the constabulary department in August, 1869; transferred to the immigration department in May, 1872; appointed a sub-agent of third class in August, 1872.
RISK, CHARLES.—Appointed road surveyor, Grenada, 1876.

RITCHIE, WILLIAM JOHNSTON.—Called to the bar of New Brunswick, 1838; created a Q.C., 1854; a member of the executive council, N.B., from October, 1854, until appointed a puisne judge of the supreme court of that province, 17th August, 1855; chief justice of New Brunswick, 6th December, 1865; a puisne judge of the supreme court of the dominion, 8th October, 1875.
RITTER, GUSTAVE A.—Clerk to the judge of the supreme court of Mauritius, 1864; marshal of vice-admiralty court.

ROACH, W. IRWIN.—Educated at Marlborough College; articled pupil, first to Messrs. J. and J. Simpson, Grosvenor engine works, London, then to A. M. Rendel, C.E., Westminster; assistant to superintendent public works, Trinidad, October, 1869; associate instit. C.E., December, 1869; colonial engineer, Grenada, February, 1872; surveyor-general and superintendent of convicts, Labuan, March, 1875;
member of legislative council, acting treasurer (in conjunction with his own office), June, 1876.
ROBERTSON, J.—Superintending officer, public works department, Ceylon, 1858.

ROBERTSON, J. ANDERSON.—Was clerk in the colonial secretary's office at Mauritius, May, 1837; chief inspector of licenses, November, 1844; coroner in admiralty court, 1846, superintendent of immigration taxes, 1847; appointed magistrate in the island, Jan. 1852; junior district magistrate, July, 1866; senior district magistrate, 1868.
ROBERTSON, J. W.—Employed on the unfixed establishment of the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, from November, 1857; assistant-surveyor, January, 1871.

ROBINSON, BRYAN.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; barrister at colonial bar of Nova Scotia and Newfoundland, 1829, at the latter of which he practised for upwards of twenty-nine years; appointed in 1858, judge of the supreme court of Newfoundland.

ROBINSON, SIR HERCULES, G.R.—G.C.M.G. (1875), (KNT. BACHEL., 1859), (K.C.M.G. 1869).—Served for some time in the 87th Fusiliers; on his retirement from the army was actively engaged during the Irish famine, 1846-9, under the commissioners of public works and poor law board in Ireland; was appointed in 1852 chief commissioner to inquire into the fairs and markets of Ireland; was appointed president of Montserrat, 1854; lieutenant-governor of St. Christopher, 1854; with which he held the dormant commission of governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands; appointed governor, of Hong Kong, June, 1859. Was a member of the commission appointed in the autumn of 1863, to inquire into the financial condition of the Straits Settlements; governor of Ceylon, 16th May, 1865; governor of New South Wales, 1872. Employed on special service in 1874-5, in connection with the cession of the Fiji Islands to Great Britain.

ROBINSON, WILLIAM, F.R.G.S.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 1st Jan. 1854; assistant junior, Nov. 3, 1854; promoted to 3rd class, June, 1858; was private secretary to Mr. Herman Merivale, from June, 1858, to May, 1860; to Lord Blachford, from May, 1860 to March, 1862; when he was promoted to be an assistant senior. Private secretary to the Right Hon. Edward Cardwell, from April 4 to June 17, 1864; appointed, Nov. 1869, to represent the Colonial Office on the East African Slave Trade Commission; senior clerk, 1st July, 1870; and a principal clerk, 30th September, 1872; appointed one of the colonial committee for the Vienna universal exhibition of 1873, and special commissioner for the Crown colonies, and superintendent of the colonial court at that exhibition; is editor of the report upon the British colonies represented there; was one of the committee for securing a permanent colonial museum in London; appointed lieutenant-governor of the Bahama Islands, Oct. 1874, and governor, 1875.

ROBINSON, WILLIAM C. F., C.M.G. (1873), —Was private secretary to Sir Hercules Robinson in the government of St. Kitts and Hong Kong, from 1855 to 1860; president of Montserrat, 1862; administered the government of Dominica from the 5th Jan. to the 18th Oct. 1865; governor and commander-in-chief of the Falkland Islands, 23rd May, 1866; governor of Prince Edward Island, 5th July, 1870, to Nov. 1873; nominated governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, 1874; appointed governor of Western Australia, 7th October, 1874.
ROCHA, J. G. DA.—Entered the post office,
2 A
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
Hong Kong, February, 1865; promoted to be accountant, July, 1872.
ROCHE, S. E.—Appointed colonial revenue officer, Carriacou, Grenada, 1864; district registrar of births and deaths, 1866; police magistrate and coroner, 1876.
ROCKWOOD, J.—Medical assistant, civil medical department in Ceylon, 1866; assistant colonial surgeon, 2nd class, 1867.

RODYK, JAMES.—Appointed second clerk to the lieutenant-governor, Penang, 24th May, 1868; acted also as chief clerk from 28th January to 21st August, 1874; appointed chief clerk to the lieutenant-governor, Malacca, 23rd Sep. 1872.

RODYK, WILLIAM.—Registrar superior court, Malacca, 8th June, 1868; entered the service of the Straits Settlements as registrar's clerk in the court of judicature at Penang on the 1st January, 1831; was transferred to Singapore, in the same department, on the 1st August, 1838, and promoted to the office of senior sworn clerk of the same court at Malacca on the 1st of May, 1848.

ROGER, CAPTAIN ARCHIBALD.—Ensign 2nd royal Lanark militia, Aug. 1859; gazetted to the 2nd West India regiment, Nov. 1860; lieutenant, Aug. 1861, captain, Oct. 1866; exchanged to the 1st battalion 17th regiment, Feb. 1867; sold out of the service, Jan. 1870; was private secretary to Mr. Bayley, governor of the Bahamas, Dec. 1860, to July, 1863, and fort adjutant, Jamaica, Dec. 1863, to Sept. 1864; appointed acting police magistrate of the rural districts, St. Christopher, Dec. 1872; confirmed in that appointment, June, 1873; appointed district magistrate, May, 1874.
ROGERS, HENRY.—Junior assistant surgeon, civil hospital, Mauritius, May, 1862; senior assistant, February, 1856.
ROLLAND, EMILE.—Assistant resident magistrate of district of Thaba Bosigo, Basutoland; appointed September, 1871.

ROLLESTON, CHRISTOPHER.—Entered the public service in New South Wales on Jan. 1, 1843, as commissioner of crown lands for the district of Darling Downs; obtained leave of absence to visit England in Jan. 1853; returned to the colony at the end of 1854; appointed private secretary to Sir William Thomas Denison, governor-general, in Jan. 1855; and registrar-general of the colony, Jan. 1, 1856; auditor-general, 1864.
RONDEAUX, J.—Appointed clerk to the second puisne judge of the supreme court, Mauritius, May, 1863.

ROOSMALECOCQ, A. H.—Appointed assistant commissioner of roads, Ceylon, 1845; assistant government agent, Galle, 1846; district judge of Tangalla, 1853; district judge, Trincomalee, 1862; ditto, Jaffna, 1869; acting district judge, Galle, May, 1873.
ROOSMALECOCQ, F.—Superintending officer public works department, Ceylon, 1850.
ROOSMALECOCQ, G. C.—Educated at University College School, London; appointed a writer in the Ceylon Civil Service, Sept. 1872.

ROSE, SIR JOHN, BART. (1872) K.C.M.G. (1870).—Was called to the bar of Lower Canada in 1842, and obtained a patent as Queen's counsel in 1847. In the year 1857 he accepted the position of solicitor-general of Canada, and was elected to parliament for the city of Montreal. Among the measures introduced by him as solicitor-general was that for the abolition of the Usury Laws. He became receiver-general of Canada in August, 1858, and afterwards minister of public works in 1859. In that capacity he had the control of the arrangements during the visit of His Royal High-

ness the Prince of Wales to Canada, and in the following year, until his resignation, those for His Royal Highness Prince Alfred. In 1867, at the first election of the Dominion parliament, he selected the county of Huntingdon, for which he was elected. The finance minister having resigned at the opening of the first session, that office, with a seat in the privy council, was accepted by him in November, 1867. He was requested by the governor-general, on behalf of Her Majesty's government, to make a confidential examination into the alleged grievances of the province of Nova Scotia to the financial terms conceded to that province in entering the union, and for a redress of which they had appealed to the Imperial Parliament. He gave it as his opinion that there were grounds for the complaints of Nova Scotia, and he recommended the extending of large financial concessions to them. He was selected in 1869 to proceed to Washington to confer with the government of the United States on the subject of reciprocal trade, the question of the fisheries, copyright, patent laws, &c., &c.; the navigation of the St. Lawrence, and extradition of criminals. In 1864 he was named by Her Majesty commissioner on the part of Great Britain, under the convention with the United States of America, of July, 1863, for the settlement of claims arising out of the Oregon Treaty of 1846, the duties of which were brought to a close in September, 1869. He resigned the office of minister of finance of Canada in October, 1869.
ROSS, A. C.—Clerk of the legislative council, and confidential clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Trinidad; entered the service in February, 1864.

ROSS, DAVID PALMER, M.D. Edin., M.R.C.S. Eng.—Chief medical officer and director, public hospital, Jamaica, 14th May, 1870; served formerly on the army medical staff; appointed parochial medical attendant, Spanish Town, 1866; medical superintendent, Cooly depôt, 1867; a government medical officer, December, 1868; acted from December, 1868, to August, 1869, as resident medical officer and registrar, public hospital, in the room of two medical officers; medical superintendent, lepers' home, from February to May, 1870.

ROSTANT, LEONARD.—Appointed warden of the Couva ward union, 1st October, 1854; warden of North Naparima ward union, 1st June, 1866; justice of the peace for the county of Victoria, 1866; supervisor of excise, 14th September, 1869.
ROUILLARD, JOHN.—District magistrate, Mauritius, February, 1865.

ROUND, FRANCIS R., B.A.—Educated at Marlborough and Balliol College, Oxford, where he obtained a double second class in moderations, and a second in classics in the final examination, 1868; appointed, after a competitive examination, clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, February, 1869; 2nd class clerk, 30th September, 1872; accompanied Sir P. Julyan, K.C.M.G., C.B., to Mauritius in 1873 as secretary on the occasion of that gentleman's special commission of inquiry into the civil establishment and general expenditure of that colony.

ROWE, SAMUEL, C.M.G. (1874), Surgeon-Major.—Engaged on the West Coast of Africa since 1862 and as medical officer and chief of the staff with Sir J. H. Glover during the Ashantee war, 1873-4, in which capacity he accompanied Sir J. H. Glover's force into Ashanti and through Coomassie; despatched on special service to the Gold Coast, November, 1874; lieutenant-governor, West Africa Settlements, 1875; administrator of the Gambia,
. COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
1875; conducted two expeditions into the Sherbro country in 1876; governor of the West Africa Settlements, 1876.
ROZARIO, RAFAEL A.—Appointed interpreter to the supreme court, Hong Kong, 1857.

RUPERT'S LAND, 2nd BISHOP OF. Appointed 1865.—RIGHT REV. ROBERT MACHRAY, D.D.—Late dean and fellow of Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge, and vicar of Madingley; graduated in 1855, when he was 34th wrangler. The diocese of Rupert's Land is 37,000 square miles in extent; and has a population of 200,000 souls, and comprehends the whole of the Hudson's Bay Company's settlements (or Prince Rupert's Land); it was erected into a see in 1849, and endowed by a legacy from the late W. Leith, Esq.

RUSHWORTH. EDW. EVERARD, C.M.G. (1872).—D.C.L. and late fellow of St. John's College, Oxford; appointed a clerk in the inland revenue office, Somerset House, 1842; was private secretary to Sir Gaspard Le Marchant, in the governments of Newfoundland and Nova Scotia, from 1847 to 1855; president administering the government of Montserrat from 1855 to 1861; auditor-general of Mauritius, 1861. Was acting director of the railway department; a member ex officio of the council, and commissioner of admiralty court in that island; appointed March, 1866, government secretary, British Guiana; administrator, government of Trinidad, April, 1866; financial secretary, Jamaica, Oct. 1866; administered the government of Jamaica during Sir J. P. Grant's six months' absence in 1870; administered the government of British Guiana during the absence of Governor Scott in 1873.
RUSSELL, THE VERY REV. A.—Dean of Adelaide.

RUSSELL, EARL (creat. first Earl, Aug., 1861) G.C.M.G. (1869).—JOHN RUSSELL.—Was M.P. for Tavistock from 1813 till Mar. 1817, and also from July, 1818, till March, 1819; for Huntingdonshire from 1820 till 1826; and for Bandon Bridge from January, 1827, till 1830; was made a privy councillor, November 22, 1830; paymaster of the forces from December 16, 1830, till December 23, 1834; M.P. for Tavistock from December, 1830, till Dec. 1832, when he was returned for South Devon, which county he represented till May, 1835; appointed one of the ecclesiastical commissioners for England, Aug. 15, 1836; secretary of state for the home department from April 18, 1835, till Aug. 30, 1839; M.P. for Stroud from May 21, 1835, till June, 1841; secretary of state for the colonies from Aug. 30, 1839, to Sept. 3, 1841; M.P. for the city of London, from July, 1841, to 1861; first lord of the treasury from July 6, 1846, till Feb. 26, 1852; secretary of state for foreign affairs from Dec. 28, 1852, till Feb. 20, 1853, when he retained a seat in the cabinet, but held no appointment; lord president of the council from June 12, 1854, till Feb. 7, 1855; appointed to be the unpaid charity Commissioner for England and Wales, July 8, 1854; sent on a special mission to Vienna, Feb. 11, 1855; appointed one of a committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, Feb. 28, 1855; secretary of state for the colonies from March to July, 1855; appointed secretary of state for foreign affairs, June 19, 1859; and one of the committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, July 6, 1859; was in attendance upon Her Majesty when she pro-
ceeded to Coburg in Sept. and Oct. 1860; prime minister, Nov. 1865, to July, 1866.

RUSSELL, JAMES.—Graduated B.A. at Queen's University, Ireland, 1863; M.A., 1873; LL.B., obtaining university gold medal, 1874; called to the bar at Lincoln's-inn, Easter term, 1874; appointed to Hong Kong cadetship, August, 1865; government interpreter, 1st December, 1867; private secretary, February, 1868; police magistrate, 1870.
RUSSELL, THE VEN. ARCHDEACON, P. T.—Vicar-general of the Roman Catholic Church, South Australia.
RUTHERFORD, G.—Collector of customs, Natal, 1853.
RUTHERFORD, W.—Employed on the unfixed establishment of the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, from 15th March, 1859; assistant surveyor, January, 1871.

ST. HELENA, 2nd BISHOP OF (founded 1859)—RIGHT REV. THOMAS EARLE WELBY, D.D.—Appointed rector of Newton, Lincolnshire, in 1847, but resigned that living on becoming a colonial chaplain at the Cape; was archdeacon of George Town, from 1856 till May, 1862, when he was consecrated bishop of St. Helena. This colony was part of the see of Cape Town previously to the 6th June, 1859, when it was constituted a diocese, together, with the islands of Ascension and Tristan d'Acunha; the population is about 7,000.

ST. HILL, RICHARD AUGUSTUS.—Native of island of St. Vincent; assistant judge of supreme court of judicature in that island; appointed, 1866 first appointment in colonial service as coroner, 1842; resigned that office in 1851; has been a justice of the peace for the island 34 years; one of a bench of magistrates selected to carry out the duties of police magistrate for the Kingston district, during the abeyance of the Act in 1850; acted as magistrate (police) for the Grenadines district in 1859; for several years an assessor and appraiser of taxable objects, &c.
ST. PERN, J. G.—Clerk to the stipendiary magistrate, Savanne, Mauritius, Sept. 1853; appointed district clerk, 1860.

SALMON, C. S.—Appointed sub-collector of customs and J. P., British Sherbro, 23rd April, 1866; acted as manager and coroner of British Sherbro from 23rd April, 1866, to 8th Feb. 1867, and from 18th Dec. 1867, to 6th March, 1868; land commissioner for Sherbro, 4th April, 1867; acted as collector of customs, Sierra Leone, from May to Oct., 1868; also as registrar for shipping; collector of customs, Gold Coast, 28th Dec., 1869; acted as chief magistrate and judicial assessor, Gold Coast, from 8th April to 2nd Nov. 1870; chief civil commissioner, Seychelles (Mauritius), 1874.

SAMUEL, SAUL, C.M.G. (1874).—Late postmaster-general of New South Wales; vice-president of the executive council and representative of the government in the legislative council of that colony; has also held office in four previous administrations as minister for finance and trade; was first elected in October, 1854, to represent the united counties of Roxburgh and Wellington in the old legislative council, before the introduction of responsible government in the colony; he afterwards represented various constituencies in the legislative assembly, his last election being for the metropolitan electorate of East Sydney, and continued in that branch of the legislature until May, 1872, when he resigned to accept a seat in the legislative council to represent the government in that house. Mr. Samuel, during his parliamentary career has been the author of several measures
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

which have become law — amongst others, the Navigation Act. In 1873, Mr. Samuel was appointed special commissioner by the government of the colony to visit New Zealand, England, and America, to make arrangements for the establishment of a mail service between the United Kingdom and the Australasian colonies by way of the United States; he resigned, 1875, with the rest of the Parkes ministry.

SANDERS, EDWARD HOWARD. — Formerly a 1st lieutenant in the Imperial Austrian 6th Dragoons, in which regiment he served for six years, leaving them in 1855 for the purpose of accepting a commission in Her Majesty's service, with the view of serving his own country in the field during the Crimean war; he was appointed lieutenant and adjutant of the 2nd Hussars, British German Legion, commanded by Lord Elphinstone, and served in them until they were disbanded; appointed stipendiary magistrate and gold commissioner of British Columbia, in April, 1859; also member of the legislative council.

SANDERSON, FREDERICK JAMES. — Appointed clerk in treasury, South Australia, 26th June, 1854; secretary to Lands Titles Commissioners, 1st Dec., 1862; and secretary to attorney-general, 1st Dec., 1870.

SANDFORD, SIR FRANCIS RICHARD, KNT. BACHEL. (creat. 1863) C.B. — Educated at Glasgow University and Balliol College, Oxford; was 1st class in classics, 1846, and graduated M.A. Oxon and LL.D. Glasgow; entered civil service as examiner privy council office, 1848, assistant secretary, 1854; knighted by patent for his services as secretary to the commissioners for the exhibition of 1862; assistant under-secretary of state for the colonies, December, 1868, to 30th January, 1870; secretary to committee of council on education, 1870.
SANDFORD, THE RIGHT REV. CHARLES WALDEGRAVE, D.D. — A bishop exercising episcopal functions over the same region as the former bishop of Gibraltar; appointed 1874.

SANGSTER, C. F. A. — Appointed clerk and accountant to the police department, Hong Kong, 1860; 2nd clerk in registrar-general's office, 1864; clerk of the supreme court, April, 1874; also deputy sheriff.

SANGUINETTI, FREDERICK S. — Is a second-class clerk in the office of the colonial secretary, Jamaica. Was appointed in Sept. 1863, a clerk in the office of the secretary to the main road commissioners; clerk in the office of the executive committee Feb. 1864; on change of constitution, appointed to present office, 16 Sept. 1866. Has acted on three occasions as a 1st class clerk.

SARGEAUNT, W. C., C.M.G. (1875). — Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1848; was permitted whilst on the establishment of that office to hold the appointment of colonial secretary of Natal from 1853 to 1858; was a member of the executive and legislative councils of that colony, a justice of the peace for the same, and lieut.-colonel of the Natal carbiniers, a corps of cavalry yeomanry; returned to the colonial office in 1858, and was appointed acting lieut.-governor of St. Vincent in 1859; returned to the colonial office, and was promoted an assistant clerk, 1860; appointed crown agent for the colonies, 1862.

SARGENT, SIR CHARLES, KNT. BACH. (1861). — Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, 1843; 5th wrangler; called to the bar, Lincoln's-inn, 1848; formerly chief justice of the Ionian Islands; now one of the puisne judges, Bombay.

SARTORIUS, MAJOR REGINALD WILLIAM, C.M.G., V.C. (1874). — 6th regiment of Bengal Cavalry, appointed to the Order of St. Michael and St. George for service in the Ashantee war, 1873-4, under Sir J. H. Glover, and received the Victoria Cross for an act of conspicuous gallantry in saving a wounded sergeant-major of Houssas.
SAULEZ, S. V. — Late lieutenant H.M.'s 103rd R. B. Fusiliers; appointed assistant-inspector Gold Coast constabulary, November, 1874; acting district commissioner Dix Cove, July, 1875.

SAUNDERS, F. R. — Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, 1857; acting assistant agent, Trincomalee, July, 1860, in which appointment he was confirmed, 1862; promoted to be assistant to government agent at Kandy, August, 1865; district judge, &c., Ratnapura, Nov. 1865; assistant to the government agent, Western Province, at Ratnapura, October 1867; acting inspector-general of police, March, 1872; acting inspector-general of prisons, Dec. 1872; confirmed, 1874.
SAVERIMOUTOU, LOUIS. — Appointed clerk in civil status office, Port Louis, Mauritius, Feb. 1, 1857.
SAVERIMOUTOU, M. — Appointed clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Mauritius, August, 1857.
SCARRISBRICK, RIGHT REV. — Roman Catholic bishop, Mauritius, 1872.

SCHMIDT, LOUIS EDWARD. — Appointed in Aug. 1861, clerk to stipendiary magistrate, Moka, Mauritius; Sept. 13, 1861, clerk in the colonial secretary's office; July, 1863, corresponding clerk; registrar, April, 1866.

SCHOLTZ, ROBERT IRWIN. — Was appointed clerk to the civil commissioner and resident magistrate of King William's Town, Cape Colony, 10th April, 1868, also distributor of stamps; appointed clerk in the deeds office at Klip Drift, Griqualand West, 5th August, 1872; chief clerk to the resident magistrate of Du Toits Pau, Griqualand West, 15th September, 1872; chief clerk to the attorney-general of Griqualand West, and clerk of the peace for the province, 10th July, 1873.

SCHÜRER, GEO. H. — Appointed clerk in the Treasury of British Honduras, July, 1869; keeper of the public cemeteries, September, 1871; private secretary to lieutenant-governor Cairns, July, 1872; performed duty as clerk to the colonial secretary of that colony for a short time, in conjunction with duties in the colonial treasury; justice of the peace, September, 1874; assistant collector of customs of the Gold Coast, November, 1874, to May, 1875; sent on special mission to Quittah, May, 1875, and received thanks of Governor-in-Chief for services in connection with the district accounts; assistant-collector and acting treasurer of Lagos, June, 1875, to January, 1876, also ex officio registrar of shipping and shipping master; commissary of taxation, British Guiana, Feb., 1876.
SCOTT, CHAS. W. — Employed as an extra clerk in the colonial office from 1840 appointed clerk in the registry department in 1852; clerk in the general department, July, 1870.

SCOTT, SIR JOHN, K.C.M.G. (1874). — Was employed in the foreign office on the affairs of the North American boundary commission, from 1840 to 1843; was appointed, in 1843, secretary to the boundary commission, established in conformity with the Ashburton Treaty of 1842, to define and mark out on the spot the boundary agreed upon in that treaty, in which operations he voluntarily undertook an active share in the explorations, surveys, and other field duties of the commission, and for which he received the special approbation of the secretary of state; at the termination of this com-
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.

mission, in 1848, was appointed surveyor-general to the new settlement of Labuan; became acting lieutenant-governor and treasurer in 1849, and was appointed lieut.-governor of the colony by commission dated Nov. 1850; in March, 1856, was transferred from the government of Labuan to that of Natal, which government he held to the end of Dec. 1865; appointed governor of British Guiana,

SCOTT, RICHARD WILLIAM, Q.C.—Born, 1825; called to the bar of Upper Canada, 1848; created a Q.C., 1867; sat in the legislative assembly of Canada from 1867 to 1873, and while there carried the separate school act of Upper Canada; returned to the Ontario Assembly at Confederation, 1867; retired on his entrance into the Dominion Government, November, 1873; called to the senate, 13th March, 1874; was speaker of the Ontario assembly from 7th to 21st December, 1871; a member of executive council and commissioner of crown lands for same province, from latter date until 7th November, 1873, when sworn of the Queen's privy council; appointed secretary of state of Canada, 9 January, 1874; is ex officio registrar-general of Canada, and has civil control of the mounted police force, enrolled for service in the north-west territories of the Dominion.
SCOTT, W. H.—Local superintendent, Mauritius railways, 20th Jan., 1864; general manager of railways, May, 1870.

SEALY, SIR JOHN, K.C.M.G. (1874), (C.M.G. 1869).—Educated at Exeter College, Oxford, where he was 2nd class in classics, 1829; graduated M.A., 1833; called to the bar at the Middle Temple, in Nov. 1833; was solicitor-general of Barbados from 1841 to 1846, when he was appointed attorney-general of that colony. Is also chancellor of the diocese; was for many years a member of the house of assembly; resigned his seat, and was appointed a member of council in 1858. Retired on a pension, 1874. Resigned seat in council, 1876.

SEALY, THOMAS.—Entered the army, May, 1841; accompanied the 10th hussars to India, 1846; from thence, via Egypt, to the Crimea; present at the fall of Sebastopol (medal and clasp and Turkish medal); entered civil service convict department, April, 1857; appointed superintendent of prisons, Trinidad, 23rd May, 1870; selected to fill the post of superintendent of the penal settlement, Massaruni, British Guiana, 26th October, 1873.

SEMPER, HUGH R.—Member of legislature, St. Kitts, 1857; member of executive council, Montserrat, 1860; called to the bar (Middle Temple), 1866; crown law officer, Tortola, 1868; attorney-general, St. Kitts, 1870; attorney-general, Leeward Islands, 1872; attorney-general, Barbados, 1874.

SEMPER, JOHN RAWLINS.—Queen's counsel, Montserrat, appointed 1844. Is a member of the executive council, solicitor-general, Antigua; chief justice of St. Kitts, Nevis, and Anguilla, 1867; and judge of the court of appeal; 1st puisne judge, Leeward Islands, 1873.
SEMPER, R.E., M.R.C.S.—Medical officer, district 6; acting president of legislative assembly St. Kitts, and member of executive council.
SENNANAYEKE, REV. C.—Appointed colonial chaplain of Morottoo, Corolawella, Ceylon, Sept, 1852; colonial chaplain, Galkisse and Millagraya, 1861; colonial chaplain, Galkisse, 1861.
SEON, WILLIAM.—Clerk to the police magistrate and petty debt court, Georgetown, British Guiana. Special justice of the peace for Georgetown.
SERRURIER, SAN CHRISTOFFEL BERRANGE.—Appointed registrar and keeper of records of the
supreme court of the Cape Good Hope, September, 1872.

SEYMOUR, JOHN FERRIS.—Appointed a clerk in the colonial secretary's office, at Mauritius, Feb. 1853; senior clerk in the customs department, Aug. 1860; chief clerk, May, 1863; officer in charge of customs, July, 1868.

SHAND, SIR CHAS. FARQUHAR, KNT. (creat. 1869).—Advocate, LL.D., Edinburgh, took academic honours every year, and repeatedly the first; author of "The Practice of the Court of Session," &c.; was an advocate depute, and counsel for the lords of the treasury, and the hon. commissioners of woods and forests in Scotland; chairman of the united industrial or ragged schools; director of the philosophical institution; and captain in the volunteer rifles, Edinburgh; appointed chief-justice of Mauritius in May, 1860; is also judge of the vice-admiralty Court.
SHARPE, HENRY.—Provost-marshal, Grenada; appointed 1855; with which office was combined, November, 1874, that of inspector of police.

SHARPE, W. E. T.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where he obtained a scholarship, and graduated B.A. 1857; appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1857; assistant government agent, Kurunégalla, 1860; and assistant agent, Kandy, 1861; promoted to district judgeship of Batticaloa, Aug. 1865; assistant Government agent, Badulla, 1868; acting government agent, north-western province, June, 1873.

SHAW, EDWARD WINGFIELD.—Retired captain royal navy, when lieutenant of H.M.S. "Plumper"; on the west coast of Africa, in 1853 and 1854, during an attack of Ashantees served on shore with Gold Coast corps as gunnery officer, and instructed that regiment in field and battery drill (their own adjutant and instructor having been killed by an explosion); for this he received the thanks of the secretary of state for the colonies; appointed private secretary to governor-in-chief of Leeward Islands in 1863; administered the government of Montserrat from April, 1865, to March, 1866; lieutenant-governor, Malacca, December, 1868.

SHAW, GILBERT.—Admitted to practice as an attorney-at-law in the supreme court of Jamaica in February, 1829; appointed clerk to the magistrates of Clarendon, 1st January, 1839; custos rotulorum of Vere, March, 1847; clerk of the peace, and magistrates' clerk of Vere, November, 1848; elected member of the house of assembly for Clarendon in December, 1859; appointed clerk of the vestry for Vere, March, 1862; served as a commissioner to inquire into and report upon the working of the immigration laws in May, 1867; appointed clerk of the Spanish Town district court, November, 1867, and provost-marshal general of the island, 1st June, 1871 (now abolished); appointed a stipendiary magistrate for St. Catherine's, July, 1874; acted as judge of the Spanish Town district court.

SHAW, GILBERT LAING.—Supernumerary clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Jamaica, April, 1866; third class clerk, 1st Sept., 1867; clerk of petty sessions for the parish of St. Catherine, 1st June, 1870.

SHAW, HENRY.—Appointed treasurer of St. Vincent, Feb. 1864; acting clerk of courts and keeper of records, 1850 (Honduras) Queen's advocate, full commission, May 30, 1851; coroner, Feb. 1857; nominee of the crown in the legislative assembly, 1861; was acting treasurer of St. Vincent from July, 1863, until his confirmation in that appointment in 1864.
SHAW, HENRY BRIDGWATER.—Inspector of
COLONIAL OFFICE LIST.
prisons, Jamaica, appointed April 1, 1863; has acted as agent-general of immigration.

SHAW, JOHN.—Late lieut. Longford militia; appointed chief clerk, customs department, 1863; acting collector, 1864, and collector and controller of customs, Sierra Leone, 1865, which office he at present holds; appointed also a commissioner for settling claims to lands, 1864; while on leave in England, during the Ashanti war, placed his services at the disposal of the secretary of state, and was by him directed to proceed to Lagos and assume the duties of collector of customs and colonial secretary in room of Captain Lees, transferred to the Gold Coast; administered the Government of Lagos from July to September, 1874; appointed treasurer of Sierra Leone, 1875.

SHEPPARD, WILLIAM TYNDALL.—Educated at Harrow and Trinity College, Cambridge (graduate, 1855); appointed clerk, railway department, South Australia, in April, 1856; clerk of court of insolvency, August, 1858; secretary to attorney-general, February, 1866; secretary to commissioner of public works, July, 1869; secretary to commissioner of crown lands and immigration, August, 1874; under treasurer, May, 1875.
SHEPSTONE, JOHN.—Assistant magistrate, P. Maritzburg, Natal, 1865.

SHEPSTONE, SIR THEOPHILUS, K.C.M.G. (1876), (C.M.G., 1869).—Appointed, Jan. 8, 1835, head-quarter interpreter of the Kafir language at the Cape, and served on the staff of Sir Benjamin D'Urban, governor and commander-in-chief, during the Kafir war of 1835; appointed clerk to the agent-general for native tribes on the frontiers of the Cape Colony, 1836; sent to accompany the first military expedition from the Cape to Natal, 1838; created justice of the peace under the Act, 6 & 7 Will. IV., to 25th degree south latitude; returned to the Cape Colony in 1839; appointed resident diplomatic agent to the Hlambe tribes, and superintendent of Fingoes at Fort Peddie, on the frontiers of Cape Colony, 1839; appointed diplomatic agent to and over the tribes inhabiting the colony of Natal, 1845; captain-in-chief of the native force in Natal, 1848; appointed to control, revise, and direct the administration of justice among the natives in that colony, according to native law, 1849; proposed and personally carried out a scheme for the taxation of the native population in the colony, which yielded about 10,000l. the first year, and now amounts to upwards of 16,000l. per annum; appointed judicial assessor Natal, under ordnance No. 1, 1855; was captain of the native police corps, from its appointment in 1848 to 1854, when it was disbanded; and has been engaged on various special missions and services in the colony not here enumerated; appointed secretary for native affairs, 1856; and member of the executive and legislative councils in that colony in the same year; proceeded on a special mission in 1873 to crown the king of Zululand; repaired to England August, 1874, to confer with the secretary of state on the native policy of the Natal government; returned in December, 1874; revisited England, July, 1876, as delegate from Natal to the London South African conference; appointed special commissioner in connection with the territories adjoining the colony, 5th Oct., 1876.

SHERIFF, ROBERT FFRENCH.—Called to the bar, Inner Temple, in November, 1862; admitted to practice at the Antigua bar in 1863; appointed crown member of the legislative council there, 1867, and one of her Majesty's counsel, 1869; administered the government of Montserrat from May,
